summary.overall: Chapter 5 was reviewed in 55 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 663 replacements, 5 splits, and 48 removals.
patterns: Answer-length cues occur when correct choices quote full regulatory language while distractors are kept artificially short. This affects both question and activity formats.
Paragraph-structure framing ('what does the note clarify') substitutes document-form trivia for operational understanding of the underlying rule.
Thin flashcard backs ('No.') defeat retrieval practice, especially when the card is reversed.

Strengths: the situation_action activity provides concrete operational context; most distractors represent plausible controller mistakes; the three key source rules each receive cross-format coverage.
Two multiple-choice questions have correct answers that are significantly longer and more specific than distractors, creating answer-length cues that reveal the answer without operational knowledge.
One true/false question has an explanation that merely names the exception rather than teaching the controlling rule or conditions.
One phraseology_builder activity has a corrupt correct_sequence array that starts with OR and never references UNAVAILABLE, making the target phrase impossible to build.
Flashcard 36080a5a contains the meta-reference 'as allowed by the paragraph' in the back field.
Word bank for phraseology_builder f43e177f contains unnecessary duplicate tokens (two AVAILABLE, two ON when only one of each is needed).

Automated negative_stem flags on activities 1e958c43, 883c8961, and d58c3a80 are false positives — these use 'Is this statement operationally correct?' instructions, which is the standard pattern for capability_check/requirement_check/conditional_rule_check activity types.

Combinatorial answer-length cue: Two items (one question, one activity) bundle multiple stop-request elements into the correct answer, making it substantially longer than any distractor and leaking the answer through length.
Thin explanation restating answer: The explanation for q_18e9ce23 repeats the correct choice without teaching the full rule including the emergency exception and ARTCC notification duty.

Near-duplicate items in same retrieval mode: Activity a_b4335088 and question 86a1e5b6 both ask 'Where are all EA activity requests referred?' in multiple-choice format — identical learning objective, wasted coverage slot.
Untested source content: The note in paragraph 5-1-3 requiring Systems Engineer consultation before approving/disapproving EA requests has no corresponding item.

Thin explanations that only restate the answer: The explanation for d2e32263 ('The general altitude threshold is 10,000 feet and above.') is nine words, merely echoes the correct choice, and does not teach the controlling rule or distinguish the altitude-based category from type-based categories.
Awkward phrasing: The back of flashcard 1154624a uses 'being unable RVSM' instead of the source phrasing 'they are unable to maintain RVSM.'

Cross-format coverage of the aircraft-category list: The three aircraft categories appear in a question (dbf11f58), an activity (4699a7cc), and a flashcard (4d31766f) — all correct. The answer-length cue flagged on the question and activity is inherent to listing three categories and does not leak the answer through design.

The pilot-request-vector rule is covered redundantly across a question (fa269a40), a requirement-check activity (a2993bb9), and a flashcard (9ea40f84) — but the retrieval modes differ enough to constitute useful cross-format reinforcement rather than harmful duplication.

The note at sequence 6 — limiting 'Unable RVSM due to turbulence (or mountain wave)' to severe turbulence with altitude deviations of approximately 200 feet or more — has no corresponding item in any format.
Answer-length cues affect four items where the correct choice contains full-source phrasing (10-18 words) but distractors are generic 4-5 word phrases, making the answer detectable by length alone.
Generic references ('this rule', 'This rule') appear in two item stems, violating the self-contained-context standard.

High cross-format redundancy: the three source directives each appear in question, activity, and flashcard formats with near-identical stems, particularly the 'when must surveillance be provided' and 'which area' items.
Explanations for most items restate the answer without adding instructional depth, though this is partially mitigated by the source being a simple procedural directive with no stated rationale.
Strong source fidelity — all items correctly reflect the source blocks.
Good cross-format reinforcement — the deviation advisory trigger and the RNAV dimensions appear in questions, flashcards, and activities.
The situation_action activity (ae636c70) is an exemplar: realistic scenario, paragraph context, plausible distractors, and an explanation that connects action to principle.
Document scaffolding ('described in the note', 'the paragraph') appears in two items and one flashcard, substituting document-section awareness for operational recall.
One activity has a genuine answer-length cue where the correct answer is noticeably longer and more specific than the distractors.
One activity uses type 'list_membership' for a task that is clearly a knowledge_check — discriminating the correct performance limit from a set of distances.
Explanations are generally adequate; a few restate the answer without teaching the wider operational context.
Answer-length cues: the correct choice combines frequency and trigger condition, making it substantially longer than distractors in three items (questions 678d2cc0, b72d6e0b and activity 2aa98608).

Thin explanations: across most items the explanation restates the answer verbatim rather than isolating the distinct rule elements (scope, frequency, precondition) that give the learner reusable knowledge.

Within-format redundancy: flashcard 674330d0 extracts only the frequency element ('At least once') which is fully subsumed by flashcard 8d2381b2 that includes both frequency and trigger condition — two cards in the same deck should not overlap this completely.
Near-duplicate stem across formats: activity 2aa98608 uses the same stem as question b72d6e0b, reducing retrieval diversity from the cross-format coverage.
Thin backs on four of six flashcards: backs of 'No.', 'It is discontinued.', 'When necessary.', and '"Radar contact."' lack the operational rule context needed for effective retrieval.
Answer-length cue: one question's correct answer embeds quoted phraseology while distractors are short and generic, telegraphing the answer.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: one question uses 'in this paragraph' and 'the paragraph' in stem and explanation, reducing the item to document trivia.
Within-format duplication: two activity items ask nearly identical true/false questions about radar contact reporting procedures.
Overloaded flashcard: one card lists all five published phraseology templates on the back while the front asks for 'one,' creating a front/back mismatch.
Thin explanations: several items restate the answer as the explanation instead of teaching the controlling rule with the source statement.
Thin back on one of six flashcards: the back of 'No.' for the IFR-cancellation-inside-named-areas exception card lacks the operational consequence that the aircraft must still be separately advised.

Thin explanation on one phraseology builder activity: the explanation restates the built phrase as a sentence instead of connecting the activity to the controlling rule and explaining why only the three-word advisory is needed.

Answer-length cue on one question: the correct phraseology answer at three words is significantly shorter than all four distractors at six to eight words, telegraphing the answer independent of knowledge.
Explanations repeatedly restate the answer without contextualizing the rule — seen in Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q6, A2, A3, A5, A6. The systemwide audit finding on explanatory thinness applies broadly here.

Three items (Q4, A3, F7) test exactly the same fact — 'beacon codes only for ADS-B and/or transponder-equipped aircraft' — in substantially the same retrieval mode. The knowledge_check activity duplicates the multiple-choice question.

Flashcard backs are frequently too thin to function as standalone retrieval targets. F4 answers 'Yes.' and F5 answers 'Computer-assigned beacon codes.' — neither reinforces the governing rule or its operational basis.
Q3's correct answer at 29 words is roughly 3x the length of its distractors, telegraphing the correct choice.
Q5 distractors use absolute, easily-rejected language ('permanently eliminates,' 'ever receiving MSAW') rather than plausible operational confusions anchored in the adjacent rules of the paragraph.
Mode 3/A terminology (F1) and the phraseology block (SQUAWK THREE/ALFA) receive minimal or zero coverage beyond a single thin flashcard.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: stems, explanations, and activity context fields embed 'under 5-2-10', 'under 5-2-11', 'under 5-2-12', and corresponding explanation references such as 'Per 5-2-10b', 'Per 5-2-11a'. These frame the question as a document lookup rather than a procedure recall, violating the self-contained-context standard.

Answer-length cue in multi-condition procedure items: items testing the two standby conditions (15-mile destination rule and clutter-reduction rule) or the Code 4000 monitoring trigger produce correct answers that are naturally longer than distractors because they restate the full rule text while distractors are single-clause negatives. Distractors need comparable operational depth.

Thin explanation on exact-phraseology recall: the fill-in-the-blank for 'SQUAWK' in the 5-2-12 transponder-reset phraseology explains only that the word is 'SQUAWK' without teaching when and why the full phraseology is used, missing the educational opportunity to anchor rote recall in operational context.

False-positive negative_stem flags: the spot_the_error, situation_action, and T/F items flagged as negative_stem are actually positively framed. The spot_the_error instruction asks the user to identify an incorrect word — that is the core mechanic of the format, not a negative constraint. The situation_action asks 'What should you do?' — an affirmative prompt. The T/F items present positive propositions.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: question stems, explanations, and activity context fields embed paragraph references (e.g., 'under 5-2-13') instead of being self-contained.
Thin explanations: many explanations restate the answer or reference a paragraph number rather than teaching the controlling rule.

Redundancy across formats: true/false questions for both paragraphs duplicate the same factual content already tested by multiple-choice questions and knowledge_check activities, adding no new retrieval mode.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the most common defect: 9 of 16 affected items have stems or explanations that cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching operational substance.
Thin and paragraph-citation explanations recur across 11 items: explanations either restate the answer verbatim or cite a paragraph reference without teaching the controlling rule.
Answer-length cues affect 5 items where correct choices are bundled lists substantially longer than single-sentence distractors.
Duplicate content: one knowledge_check activity (aa4dfd90) is functionally identical to a multiple-choice question (beb9144b) with the same stem and choices.
Incomplete correct answers: two items (beb9144b, aa4dfd90) list only three of four validation triggers from 5-2-15a, omitting interfacility handoff.

Strong items: the readback_check activities (cdeec27b, b1761e5f) are well-designed with plausible readback distractors. The situation_action items provide solid operational context. Both flashcards are concise and accurate.
Negative-stem heuristics are over-reported by automated flags; the negative framing is appropriate for prohibitions (5-2-15c) and exclusion recognition (5-2-16 exceptions).
Every question stem includes a paragraph reference ('under 5-2-XX') that teaches document-navigation trivia instead of ATC operational substance.
Every question explanation cites a paragraph number ('Per 5-2-XX') instead of teaching the controlling rule in operational terms.

Activity items (knowledge_check, readback_check, situation_action, spot_the_error) are consistently self-contained and avoid paragraph-location scaffolding — a clear quality gap between question and activity generation.

Flashcard for 5-2-19 has a front that asks when approval may be granted but a back that only covers the negative denial case, omitting the positive approval conditions (traffic-based, IFR/VFR prerequisites).
One fill_blank question drops the period after NEGATIVE in the prescribed phraseology, slightly degrading phraseology precision.
True/false questions test discrete knowledge boundaries appropriately but would benefit from explanations that teach the full operational context rather than single-fact confirmation.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: three 5-2-20 items embed 'under 5-2-20' or 'Per 5-2-20' in stems or explanations; one activity's para_context does the same.
Thin explanations: several items restate the answer ('That is the basic restriction stated in the paragraph') instead of teaching the controlling rule and its exceptions.

Paragraph-scope trivia: one question tests which code the paragraph is 'concerned with' and one flashcard asks what the paragraph 'specifically regulates' — both teach document navigation instead of operational substance.
Answer-length cue: one question has a correct answer substantially longer than any distractor, revealing the answer by length alone.
Duplication: one activity (knowledge_check) is a near-verbatim copy of a question from the same paragraph — same stem, same correct answer, similar distractors.
Weak/implausible distractor: one activity uses 'Resume normal speed' which is unrelated to transponder operations.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: seven questions and one activity embed 'under 5-2-XX' or 'Per 5-2-XX' in stems, explanations, or para_context fields. These citations teach document navigation rather than operational substance and violate the self-contained stem requirement.

Thin explanations: several question explanations restate the correct answer verbatim ('Per 5-2-21, the upper limit is set no lower than 1,000 feet above the highest altitude...') without teaching the broader operational context.

Duplicate retrieval: question 3dba9b0c is a verbatim fill_blank duplicate of bd55a1bf, testing the same phraseology word SQUAWK in the same format. The phraseology appears in both 5-2-22 and 5-2-23 source blocks.
Answer-length cue: the correct answer in activity 376172c3 is substantially longer than any distractor, revealing the answer by length before the learner reads its content.

Fluency strengths: activities across all three paragraphs use diverse formats (knowledge_check, situation_action, readback_check, spot_the_error) with well-chosen incorrect tokens and plausible operational distractors. The flashcards are correctly scoped and balanced.
Paragraph-location scaffolding persists in stems and explanations across both paragraphs despite the automated flags.
Two questions exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than uniform short distractors.
Explanations for phraseology items are circular — they restate the target phrase without conveying when or why the phraseology applies.
The shard includes one near-verbatim duplicate: question 1cc1ea53 and activity 1f2916bd share the same stem and retrieval mode.
Flashcard a9b89c2b conflates two distinct operational duties (pilot-request response and authorized-OFF notification prohibition) into one back.
The true/false items and situation-action activities are well-constructed with plausible operational distractors.
Cross-format reinforcement is present for the 7700-assignment rule and the discrete-code-change exception, which is a strength.
Thin explanations: all five 5-2-4 questions and two of five 5-2-4 activities restate the answer rather than teach the operational requirement and the source basis for it.

Concept redundancy: the 7600-display-requires-communications-failure-procedures concept appears in five items (two MCQ questions, two activities, one flashcard) in near-identical retrieval modes. Removed one question, one activity, and one flashcard.
Flashcard backs reduced to bare abbreviations ('RF.', 'RDOF.') without sufficient context to be self-contained.
5-2-3 flashcards are uniformly well-formed: self-contained fronts and backs, appropriate card types (exception, phraseology, requirement), and operationally relevant content.
7700 display labels (ERAM: EMRG, STARS/MEARTS: EM) from 5-2-3 note seq 2 are absent from all learning formats despite being analogous to the tested 7600 display labels.
Source-constrained redundancy: three short source blocks forced into 15 targets created 3-4 items per format testing the identical body-block directive.
Thin explanations and backs: every explanation restates the answer because the source states only actions and labels without operational rationale.
System-label fragmentation: ERAM and STARS/MEARTS display labels are split across separate questions and flashcards; the list_membership activity (4c6f6120) models the better combined approach.
True/false used as format filler: two true/false items (one question, one activity) at difficulty 1-2 duplicated the concept without adding retrieval diversity or application depth.
Negative stem correctly justified: the scope_check activity (a9ab1d99) uses a negative frame that directly tests exclusion recognition — distinguishing 7500 from 7600 and 7700 is the intended skill.

Situation_action is the strongest format in this shard: activity 214e8041 applies the rule in a contextual scenario with plausible distractors, modeling the kind of operational application preferred over verbatim recall.
Answer-length cue: correct answers that are long, specific document references or compound lists stand out against short, generic distractor choices.
Near-duplicate question/activity pairs: three activities are clones of questions testing the same fact with near-identical stems and answer sets, adding no retrieval diversity.
Thin or restating explanations: several explanations repeat the answer verbatim without teaching the controlling source step, cross-reference, or exception.
Flashcard format drift: one card is labelled 'definition' but carries a five-item procedural list; another card's back is an acronym with no operational context.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the primary defect across all three paragraphs — stems and explanations reference paragraph numbers that the learner should not need to memorize.
Several automated 'negative_stem' flags are false positives for this shard; the flagged stems (cancel/termination scenario, spot-the-error activity) are not negatively constructed.
5-2-7 Note 2 (VFR glider squawk 1202) and Note 3 (formation lead squawk 1203) have zero learning coverage in this shard — they appear in the source but are absent from all three formats.
5-2-8 Note 2 (code preset on ground, crew may not accept changes) has zero coverage — it is an operationally important exception that should be covered.
Cross-format reinforcement is limited; most source statements map to exactly one or two formats.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is pervasive: 7 of 9 questions embed a paragraph reference in the stem, making items depend on document-navigation knowledge instead of operational understanding. Confirm by inspecting every question stem for 'under 5-X-Y' or similar.
Paragraph-number explanations: multiple items teach 'Per 5-X-Y…' rather than stating the rule itself as the controlling principle.
Answer-length cue in one question and one activity: the correct answer is substantially longer and more specific than the distractors, revealing the answer without operational knowledge.
Flashcards across all three paragraphs are well-constructed: self-contained fronts, concise backs listing methods, no paragraph scaffolding.
One item is pure document-trivia — the correct answer is paragraph numbers — teaching the location of exceptions instead of their operational content.
The spot-the-error activity correctly tests phraseology recognition for SQUAWK STANDBY vs IDENT, and the readback-check provides operationally relevant pilot readback practice.

Near-duplication between question 9bb2dded and activity 5e4f7aaf (both ask 'what must be established/maintained before radar service') in essentially the same retrieval mode, though different distractors and entity types partially mitigate the overlap.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect, appearing in 12 of 19 items. Stems and explanations systematically embed 'under 5-3-X' or 'Per 5-3-X' instead of teaching the operational rule.
Explanations are routinely thin citation proxies: 'Per 5-3-4c', 'That rule summarizes 5-3-4a', 'That requirement appears in 5-3-4c'. These teach nothing about the operational principle.
Compound rules produce answer-length cues when the correct choice quotes the two-condition rule verbatim and distractors remain one-line operational mistakes.

Items without paragraph scaffolding are structurally sound: the knowledge_check activities for 5-3-5 and 5-3-6 work well, the list_membership activity for data block codes is effective, and the compound-rule flashcards correctly balance front/back.

Cross-format coverage is uneven: 5-3-4 has four activities and one flashcard, while 5-3-5 has only one activity. The source block on en route primary target display (5-3-5b2) appears only in one true/false question.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect, appearing in 15 of 23 items. Stems, explanations, and activity para_context fields systematically embed 'under 5-3-X' or 'Per 5-3-X' instead of posing the operational question directly.

Two items are near-duplicate retrieval targets: the en route and terminal data block retention questions test an identical rule worded identically in both paragraphs; the knowledge_check activity on minimum data block display repeats the same question as a multiple_choice item.
One flashcard (cda89fc0) overloads a single card with three distinct recall targets: retention duration, minimum display content, and the ERAM full-data-block condition.

The readback_check activity for RADAR CONTACT LOST (cf54f783) is well-constructed — it tests phraseology in its proper format with plausible operational distractors. The situation_action activities for 5-3-7 and 5-3-8 are also structurally sound once paragraph references are stripped.
The 5-3-9 VFR exception is tested by both a true/false question and a knowledge_check activity, providing appropriate reinforcement for a note-based exception.
One flashcard (19755817) weakens the mandatory transfer requirement by using permissive 'should' instead of the source's 'must.'
The 5-3-7 flashcard (249980d4) is sound — it tests a coherent pair of related advisories in a single compact card without overloading.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the most widespread defect, appearing in stems prefixed with 'Under 5-4-X' and explanations starting with 'Per 5-4-X'. This trains document-structure recall instead of operational decision-making.
Explanations frequently restate the answer or cite a paragraph number rather than teaching the controlling operational rule, condition, or exception.
One source fidelity gap: the Point Out Approved definition in question 0fedfa27 omits 'or the appropriate automated system response' from the source text.
One cross-format near-duplicate: activity 6eb06ea7 and question a594af0b are nearly identical in stem, answer, and distractors.
The correct answer for the handoff definition question (54ed0995) is substantially longer than distractors, creating a length cue.
Spot-the-error and phraseology-builder activities are well-constructed with accurate source material.
All three flashcards are concise and accurate.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect, affecting 14 of 20 items. Stems prepend 'Under 5-4-X' and explanations embed 'Per 5-4-Xa,' teaching paragraph-number recall instead of operational understanding.

Thin explanations affect 5 items where the explanation merely names a paragraph identifier or restates the answer in insufficient detail without teaching the controlling rule, exception, or operational condition.

Answer-length cues arise in 3 items where the correct choice expresses a compound rule (condition plus exception) while distractors are simple declarative statements, revealing the answer independent of knowledge.

Near-duplication across question and activity formats inflates the item count without genuine cross-format reinforcement. Two items use identical stems to test 5-4-5b, varying neither the operational angle nor the tested skill.

Coverage gaps are notable in paragraph 5-4-5: subparagraphs c (advise pertinent info), e (verbal coordination for CST/FAIL/NONE/IF/NT/TRK), and h (pre-communication-transfer duties) are entirely unrepresented despite containing operationally critical checklists.
STRENGTH: All correct answers are factually accurate against source blocks — no source-fidelity errors in stems or answer keys beyond the one flashcard back.
STRENGTH: Situation-action activities (059b6885, 1bb649d4, 01680590) provide good operational-application coverage and only need paragraph-reference scrubbing.

DEFECT: Paragraph-location scaffolding is nearly universal — 8 of 9 questions and 5 of 8 activities embed paragraph references in stems, prompts, or explanations, teaching document navigation instead of ATC substance.

DEFECT: Four activities duplicate the retrieval mode and concept of corresponding MC/TF questions (91186325↔784e268e, 8e8cd4c4↔d1367a99, 99e9ce47↔36f30ed8, ac13f713↔d9701eb4). These knowledge_check/requirement_check activities offer no retrieval diversity.
DEFECT: Answer-length cue in 99e9ce47 — the correct answer is a compound condition while distractors are single-clause statements, making the answer guessable by length alone.
DEFECT: Flashcard deb816d7 (5-4-7) includes back content not present in the source blocks, requiring a full back replacement.
DEFECT: Question a5ca6ceb asks which option 'is listed in the 5-4-9 note' — a document-trivia premise that must be restructured into an operational coordination question.

GAP: The 5-4-9 note content about coordination methods and the prohibition on uncoordinated airspace entry is covered only in T/F format; a multiple-choice question testing selection of proper coordination methods in context would strengthen coverage.

GAP: The 5-4-7 receiving-controller responsibilities (b1–b3: target correspondence, separation responsibility, issuing restrictions) are tested by only one scope_check activity (e7c671d5); additional coverage in varied formats is warranted.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 12 of 19 items embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context fields, teaching document trivia rather than operational recognition of when rules apply.
Weak explanations: Most explanations restate the answer prefixed with a paragraph citation instead of teaching the controlling condition, exception, or operational principle.
Uneven subparagraph coverage: 5-5-1b3's five mandatory conditions for mixed radar/nonradar separation receive zero coverage, while 5-5-1b2 is tested by three items across two formats.
Answer-length cue: One item has a correct answer substantially longer than its distractors, making the answer identifiable without operational knowledge.

Reference-trivia activity: The table_lookup activity asks students to identify which reference table is cited, testing document navigation rather than the operational significance of equipment suffixes.
Paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-5-X', 'Per 5-5-X') infects stems and explanations across 11 of 17 items, the single most prevalent defect.

Both true/false items (5-5-12 correction factor, 5-5-2 digital display separation) duplicate content already tested by equivalent multiple-choice questions; each T/F merely restates the source text verbatim.

Flashcard 7e0e514c (5-5-2) omits sub-rule c (end of beacon control slash to center of primary target) and the no-touching restriction from sub-rule a, giving an incomplete picture of target separation measurement.

5-5-3 sub-rules c1-c3 (edges of primary targets, end of slash to edge of primary, ends of two slashes for target resolution) have zero items. Only the definition, advisory requirement, and mosaic prohibition are covered.
Thin explanations in two activities (963e7795, 03f19579) restate the answer rather than connecting the answer to the operational condition described in the source.

Strength: distractors across all multiple-choice items are operationally plausible (confusing displacement maximum with correction factor, confusing target resolution rules with target separation rules, reversing the mosaic restriction).
Strength: the situation_action activities provide realistic operational scenarios with specific display contexts.

Paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations is the most common defect across all formats — about three-quarters of items embed paragraph references rather than teaching the operational rule independently.
True/false questions restate source text verbatim, testing recognition of a quoted rule rather than operational understanding or application.
Answer-length cues occur where the correct answer carries necessary operational detail that distractors lack, creating a structural reveal.
Flashcard backs are sometimes thin ('5 NM') or vague ('under the paragraph's standards'), missing the operational context that would make the retrieval target meaningful.
One question asks how aircraft may be vertically separated but lists only one of two valid source methods as the correct answer, creating ambiguity.

Strengths: distractors are operationally plausible mistakes, situation-action activities provide realistic scenarios with reasonable judgment elements, and table_lookup activities correctly identify source tables with specific lookup tasks.

Paragraph-location scaffolding in stems, explanations, para_context, and source_basis — the most frequent defect across the shard. Items cite 'under 5-5-X' or 'per 5-5-X' instead of framing the operational condition.
Thin explanations that restate the answer or cite a paragraph instead of teaching the controlling rule, condition, or exception.
Answer-length cue in one activity: the correct choice is substantially longer than three short distractors, making it identifiable without domain knowledge.

Four items across three formats cover the same +1-mile standard-formation rule (5-5-8a) — acceptable cross-format reinforcement but leans heavy on one sub-rule while nonstandard formation perimeter separation (5-5-8c) has zero coverage.
The en route pilot-report passing/diverging procedure (5-5-7b) with its five cumulative conditions is not tested by any item in this shard.
Obstruction separation coverage omits single-sensor MSSR (5-5-9a3) and STARS Multi-Sensor Mode (5-5-9a5) minima.
Paragraph-location scaffolding in 17 of 22 items (stems and/or explanations cite paragraph numbers), the primary defect.
Answer-length cues in 7 multiple-choice items where the correct answer substantially outruns distractors.
Thin or citation-only explanations in 4 items that merely name the paragraph or restate the answer without operational context.
True/false items that restate source text verbatim, removing the need for operational judgment.
Strong items: the 5-6-2 phraseology_builder (0f96cdbf), 5-6-1 flashcard (1cb09555), and 5-6-2 procedure flashcard (abd3b5f1) are well-formed.
Paragraph-location scaffolding in roughly half of stems and explanations — the most frequent defect, present across both paragraphs and all entity types.
Thin explanations that cite a paragraph label ('Per 5-7-1e') without teaching the operational rule or its application.
One answer-length cue where the correct multi-principle answer is substantially longer than single-clause distractors.
One set of implausible distractors that no controller would ever consider issuing, providing no educational value.
One near-duplicate of the FL390 consent rule between a question and a list-membership activity.
Scenario-based activities (f698a28d, 442e61ad) are strong and need only paragraph-reference removal.
Phraseology items — the fill-blank, phraseology builder, readback check, and spot-the-error — are well-constructed with only explanation-quality issues.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: all 10 question stems and nearly all explanations embed paragraph citations ('Under 5-7-3', 'Per 5-7-4c', 'That requirement appears in 5-8-1b'). This substitutes document-trivia signposts for operational teaching.

Thin explanations that cite a paragraph label without teaching the operational rule, conditions, or application — most severe in T/F items where the explanation is simply 'That assignment appears in 5-7-3e' or 'That note appears under 5-7-4a'.
One answer-length cue: the correct answer in a020146b is substantially longer than the three distractors, making the answer identifiable without content knowledge.
One flashcard source-fidelity error: 42731c21 adds 'arrival' to the front, narrowing a rule that applies to all aircraft beneath Class B airspace or in a VFR corridor.
One multi-rule flashcard: 08429d76 packs two distinct 5-8-1 rules (concurrent DVA prohibition and published heading range limits) into a single back.
The two situation_action activities (df5dcc65, 5664c62d) are well-constructed operationally and need only paragraph-reference removal from their context and explanation fields.

Phraseology items — the fill-blank (fded4369) and knowledge checks about RESUME NORMAL SPEED and RESUME PUBLISHED SPEED — test the right retrieval targets but need operational explanations instead of paragraph citations.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: 12 items embed 'Under 5-8-X' in the stem or 'Per 5-8-X' in the explanation, teaching document structure instead of operational substance.
Thin explanations: 6 items provide explanations that either restate the answer verbatim or merely cite a paragraph number without teaching the controlling operational principle.
True/false overuse: 3 of 8 questions use true/false format for rules that would benefit from scenario-driven multiple-choice framing. Two of these are flagged for paragraph-location explanations.
Answer-length cueing: 2 knowledge-check activities have correct answers substantially longer than distractors, making the correct choice identifiable without operational knowledge.
Underspecified list-membership: 1 activity asks 'Which departure type is addressed by this separation rule?' without presenting the rule, making the task impossible without guessing the referent.
Flashcard quality: All 3 flashcards are well-formed, self-contained, and free of the scaffolding defects present in other formats.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the single most pervasive defect, affecting 13 of 19 items. Stems lead with 'Under 5-X-Y' and explanations open with 'Per 5-X-Ya,' substituting document trivia for self-contained operational framing.

Thin explanations are concentrated in true/false items and fill-in-the-blank items, where explanations default to 'That statement appears directly in...' or 'Per 5-X-Y, the prescribed phraseology uses...' rather than teaching the controlling rule.
Answer-length cue in one knowledge_check activity: the correct weather-conditional answer is substantially longer than the three distractors, making the answer identifiable by length alone.
Two flashcards contain context-light back text ('in the paragraph,' 'the listed weather') that assumes access to the source document.

Cross-format reinforcement is present for the 30-degree divergence rule (question + activity + flashcard all cover it) and the 2-mile approach gate vector rule (question + activity + flashcard). Other source statements in the shard lack multi-format coverage.

Negative-stem flags on items where the concept inherently involves negation (e.g., 'does not waive,' 'do not acknowledge'). Most of these are false positives; the exclusion IS the concept being taught. Only one question (63a68523) had a genuinely awkward negative construction and was rephrased.

Several stems begin with paragraph references rather than the operational condition.

Some correct options are much longer than distractors.
Paragraph-location scaffolding ('Under 5-14-7,' 'Per 5-14-8b') appears in 10 of 14 items across stems, explanations, and para_context fields, training document navigation instead of rule application.
Explanations uniformly defer to the source paragraph reference rather than stating the operational requirement directly.
One activity has a pronounced answer-length cue where the correct choice is substantially longer than all distractors.
Source fidelity is high: every item faithfully reflects its source block without factual distortion.
The two unaffected items (f1b4c9db, d2b009de) demonstrate the target quality: self-contained stems with direct, operational explanations.
generation_guidance: Frame every stem around the operational decision, condition, or requirement — never around document structure ('the note says', 'paragraph X states').
Balance distractor length against the correct answer. When the correct answer must quote regulatory language, write distractors of comparable specificity and length.
Flashcard backs must contain enough substance to stand as a retrieval target in both directions. A back of 'No.' or 'True.' fails this test.

For thin source paragraphs like 5-1-1, avoid manufacturing rationale not present in the source. The adequacy judgment, commissioning-parameter exception, and malfunction-notification requirement each stand alone without additional 'why' in the source text.

Balance answer and distractor lengths so correct answers are no longer than the longest distractor. When a correct answer must be long because it encodes two related rules, either split the item or extend distractors to comparable length.
Write explanations that teach the operational rule, condition, or exception. Do not merely name or restate the answer. Reference the specific source provision the item tests.
Verify phraseology_builder correct_sequence arrays against target_phrase before committing. The sequence must produce the exact target phrase from the word bank.
Avoid meta-references to paragraph structure in flashcard backs (e.g., 'as allowed by the paragraph'). The back should contain only the operational answer.

For this paragraph's key provisions: use multiple-choice questions for conditional application (when/in which modes), flashcards for core definitions and lists, phraseology_builder for exact advisory wording, and requirement/conditional_rule checks for prohibition recognition.

Do not flag standard activity instructions ('Is this statement operationally correct?') as negative stems. The negative_stem heuristic should be limited to stems that negate the tested concept rather than instructions that invite a true/false judgment.
For stop request content questions, test one element at a time to avoid answer-length cues. Match the format: a list_membership activity should ask about a single list element.
When writing explanations for procedural rules, teach the complete rule including its conditions and exceptions — not just the answer to the specific question asked.

Check for near-duplicate items across formats during generation. When a question and an activity test the identical fact, repurpose one to cover untested source content (e.g., the Systems Engineer consultation note).
The phraseology_builder activity (5fdfc039) and the phraseology recognition question (518ab01e) provide good cross-format reinforcement of the BIG PHOTO / STOP BUZZER phraseology without duplication.

Short paragraphs like 5-1-3 can still support diverse retrieval; use en route vs. terminal distinctions, stop vs. resume procedures, and note content to generate distinct items rather than recycling the same surface facts.

When writing explanations for simple threshold questions, go beyond restating the answer. Teach the controlling rule from which the threshold derives, and distinguish the threshold-based category from other categories in the same rule.

Verify flashcard back text against the exact source phrasing for prescribed or semi-prescribed language like 'unable to maintain RVSM.' Awkward compression ('being unable RVSM') degrades the card's value as a retrieval target.

The note at sequence 6 (severe turbulence threshold of approximately 200 feet altitude deviation) is substantively untested. A conditional-rule activity or requirement-check question covering when the 'Unable RVSM' report actually applies would fill this gap.
The phraseology_builder activity (2ccdfd18) and phraseology flashcard (274ceaa8) provide good cross-format reinforcement of the vector-for-traffic phraseology without duplication.

The T/F question (7dece90a) and the conditional-rule-check activity (823b710f) are strong formats for testing the exact scope and conditions of the rule paragraphs. Future generation should favor these formats for conditional rules.

For narrow paragraphs like 5-1-5 with only two or three distinct directives, reduce total item count rather than producing near-duplicate stems across formats. Accept fewer items that are higher quality.

Balance distractor length to match the correct answer length. When the correct answer must use precise FAA terminology, budget extra words for distractors so the length differential does not reveal the answer.
Never use 'this rule', 'this paragraph', or 'this section' initem stems or flashcard fronts. Identify the operational concept directly.

When a source block offers no explanatory rationale, teach the requirement through conditions and scope rather than inventing purpose claims. Restating the answer is acceptable for simple directives if the restatement includes the full condition set.

Vary retrieval angles across formats for the same directive: one item can test the 'when' condition, another the 'where' scope, and a third the 'what action follows' — rather than re-asking the identical stem in multiple formats.

Never embed document-structure references ('the note', 'the paragraph', 'this section') in question stems, activity instructions, flashcard fronts, or explanations. The student should recall the rule, not the location.
Balance choice lengths to prevent answer-length cues. When the correct answer is a source phrase, ensure distractors have comparable specificity and word count.
Match activity_type to the actual cognitive skill: knowledge_check for single-best-answer discrimination, list_membership only when the task is to judge membership in a defined set.

When an explanation can link two related rules (e.g., the 2-mile performance limit and the 4-mile protected airspace), include that connection to deepen operational understanding rather than treating each number in isolation.
True/false items should test genuine understanding, not near-verbatim recognition of a source sentence. Frame the statement in an applied or slightly reformulated way.
For rules stated as compound conditions (trigger + minimum + scope), keep each multiple-choice distractor comparably specific and comparably long so the correct answer does not stand out by length.

When the source is a single-sentence operational duty, an explanation should separately identify the scope, frequency, precondition, and target — not just repeat the sentence. This helps learners recognize which element changes when the rule is tested from a different angle.

Flashcards from very short paragraphs are prone to atomization into single-word backs. Err toward cards that name a concrete retrieval target (e.g., the trigger condition by itself, the scope by itself) and ensure that every back is a useful fact, not a single word that the front already implies.

Do not generate a knowledge-check activity whose stem and answer set are identical to a question targeting the same paragraph. When the same retrieval is delivered in both an activity and a question, change the framing (situational prompt, conditional prompt, error-recognition) so the two formats practice different cognitive angles.
For flashcards with exact-phrase answers, include at minimum one sentence of operational context after the phrase so the back teaches, not just displays.
Ensure distractor length and specificity match the correct answer to avoid answer-length cues; all choices should read as comparably plausible operational statements.
Remove all paragraph/section/document deictic references from stems, question_text, and explanations — the item must be self-contained.
When a flashcard back must list multiple phraseology templates, split into categorized cards focused on a single retrieval target each (e.g., fix-based vs. airway/NAVAID-based).
Avoid placing the same true/false concept in two activities of the same paragraph; if cross-format reinforcement is needed, use a question or flashcard instead.
For 'when necessary' or similarly brief rule conditions, explain that the condition establishes controller discretion rather than simply echoing the qualifying phrase.
For flashcards whose back is a bare denial ('No.'), include the operational consequence: what the controller must do instead of relying on automatic termination.

For phraseology builder activities, the explanation should reference the controlling rule that mandates the phraseology and note whether the scenario calls for the full transmission or a subset; do not simply echo the built phrase.

For phraseology-recall questions, ensure distractor length and visual weight match the correct answer so students cannot eliminate choices by shape alone; use comparably concise, all-caps incorrect phraseology candidates as distractors.
Continue avoiding paragraph-number references, invented safety rationales, and document-location trivia — the current generation already does this cleanly across all sixteen items.

Write explanations that teach WHY a rule exists when the source states a reason (e.g., ARTCC code supports auto-acquisition), and teach WHAT the rule requires when the source states only a requirement. Never pad with 'this ensures safety' or 'this is important because' unless the source block says so.
Ensure flashcard backs contain enough substance to reinforce retrieval — a single word or a bare 'Yes' is insufficient even for yes/no cards. Include the key requirement or the operational context.

Before generating a knowledge_check activity alongside a multiple_choice question, verify the two items are testing the concept in genuinely different ways. If both present the same stem with the same choices, keep only one format.

Balance correct-answer length against distractor lengths. When the correct answer must be long because the rule is wordy, lengthen the distractors with similarly specific (but wrong) operational detail rather than leaving them short and generic.

Test Code 4000 flight categories more broadly — the note lists five categories (flight test, MTR, aerial refueling, ALTRVs, special handling) but only flight test appears in the current activity. A matching or classification activity covering the full list would improve coverage.
The SQUAWK THREE/ALFA phraseology and the discrete-code-depletion exception are untested in this shard. Consider items for these topics in a later pass.

Do not embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, para_context fields, or instruction text. Reference the procedure by its operational name or situation, never by its document location. An item should be answerable by a controller who knows the rule, not by one who knows the table of contents.

When a correct answer describes two or more conditions drawn from a single paragraph, write distractors of comparable length and operational specificity. Do not pair a multi-condition correct answer with single-clause negative distractors. The rule's complexity should not leak the answer through length alone.

For fill-in-the-blank items testing prescribed phraseology, the explanation must teach the full phraseology and its operational trigger. Do not write a one-sentence explanation that only names the missing word. Connect the rote recall to the controller action it supports.
For readback_check and spot_the_error activities, explanations should reference the phraseology by its operational purpose — e.g., 'when placing the transponder in standby' — not by paragraph number.

Avoid the word 'only' in flashcards unless the source rule is genuinely exhaustive. The standby conditions in 5-2-10 are permissive ('You may instruct'), not exclusive. Cards may list the authorized cases but should not imply no others exist unless the paragraph says so.

When generating situation_action activities that synthesize two sub-paragraphs (e.g., Code 4000 monitoring PLUS VFR-code discontinuation), ensure the correct answer addresses both conditions explicitly and neither half is hinted by answer-length disparity.
Write stems and context fields that are self-contained without paragraph-number knowledge. Omit 'under 5-X-Y' and similar references from all item fields.
Ensure explanations teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception directly. For narrow directives, state the requirement clearly without inventing unsupported rationales.

Avoid adding true/false items that test the same single fact already covered by a multiple-choice question or knowledge_check activity. Reserve true/false for exclusion judgments or conditional discriminations not otherwise assessed.
For readback_check activities, write explanations that address why the correct readback is correct (e.g., preserving prescribed wording) rather than restating the controller's underlying duty.
Eliminate all paragraph-number citations from question stems. Students must recognize operational triggers without artificial cues about which paragraph contains the answer.
Teach the controlling rule in every explanation, not just the paragraph location. For prohibitions (do not use unvalidated Mode C for separation), explain what the controller must do instead.
Avoid bundled-list correct answers. If the list itself is the retrieval target, split it into distinct items. Otherwise, ask about a single trigger or exception in a focused scenario.
Ensure correct answers covering enumerated source lists include every item from the source. The 5-2-15a validation triggers consist of four categories; answers listing only three are incomplete.

Distinguish operational decision items from rote-list items. Where the source enumerates conditions, ask the student to recognize whether a given scenario triggers the rule rather than to recite the complete enumeration.

For readback_check activities, the explanation should state why the correct readback matches the clearance elements and why each distractor fails on a specific element (wrong altimeter, wrong phraseology, wrong response type).

Cross-validate items across formats to prevent near-duplicates: when a knowledge_check activity and a multiple-choice question share identical stems and choices, one of them should test a different operational angle.
The terminal-only intrafacility exception (5-2-16d) should be tested distinctly from the three universal exceptions so students recognize its facility-type constraint.
Eliminate all paragraph-number references ('under 5-2-XX', 'per 5-2-XX') from question stems and explanations. Replace with the operational situation or rule naming.
Write explanations that teach the controlling rule, conditions, and exceptions rather than citing a paragraph number. The explanation should stand alone as a teaching tool.
For prescribed phraseology items, preserve exact punctuation and formatting from the source phraseology blocks — including periods, parentheses, and capitalization.
Flashcard fronts and backs must answer each other directly. When a front asks 'when may X,' the back must state the positive conditions, not only the negative prohibition.
Continue the strong activity construction pattern seen in this shard: self-contained situations, plausible operational distractors, and scenario-context that tests decision-making rather than recall.
When a single paragraph has multiple sub-rules (like 5-2-19 a through f), ensure flashcard coverage spans the primary decision framework rather than only one subparagraph.
Strip every 'under 5-X-Y' and 'Per 5-X-Y' from stems, explanations, and activity content fields. The item must be self-contained without paragraph-number knowledge.

Explanations must teach the controlling operational rule and its conditions/exceptions, not restate the answer or cite the paragraph number. For 5-2-2: explain the default timing restriction and three exception mechanisms. For 5-2-20: explain that STOP SQUAWK is the prescribed phraseology to instruct the pilot to turn off transponder and ADS-B Out transmission, with the military variant used when the mode in use should be specified.
Keep all choices at similar length when testing multiple-choice items; a correct answer that is visibly longer than distractors reveals itself by length alone.
Do not generate paragraph-scope questions ('What code is this paragraph concerned with?') or flashcards ('What does this paragraph regulate?'). Test operational substance, not document structure.

When generating items for a paragraph with limited operational content (like 5-2-2 which has one core rule with exceptions), vary the retrieval angle rather than duplicating near-identical items in multiple formats.
Distractors must be plausible operational mistakes related to the topic. For transponder/beacon items, unrelated distractors like 'Resume normal speed' are not viable.
Flashcard backs must be substantive enough for retrieval. A back that says only 'No.' fails to reinforce the underlying rule. For exception-type cards, include the governing rule in the back.

The readback_check and spot_the_error activity types are well-suited to 5-2-20's phraseology content. Ensure their explanations connect the answer to the operational principle, not just the paragraph number.
Strip every 'under 5-X-Y' and 'Per 5-X-Y' from stems, explanations, and activity content fields. Items must be self-contained without paragraph-number knowledge.

Explanations must teach the controlling operational rule and its conditions, not restate the answer with a paragraph citation. For 5-2-21: explain the upper and lower filter buffers, the field elevation rule, and the clutter exception. For 5-2-22: explain the three-part response to an inoperative ADS-B transmitter (notify OS/CIC with three elements, inform pilot with exceptions, stop transmissions only when ATC functions are jeopardized). For 5-2-23: explain the CSMM trigger condition and the escalation from inform to stop-and-notify when ATC functions are jeopardized.
Keep all multiple-choice answers at comparable length. When the correct answer requires stating multiple requirements, compress it to its essentials and ensure distractors are similarly substantive.

Do not generate a duplicate item when the same phraseology or rule appears in adjacent paragraphs. If the phraseology 'STOP ADS-B TRANSMISSIONS, AND IF ABLE, SQUAWK THREE/ALFA (code)' is already tested in a 5-2-22 item, test 5-2-23 through its unique content: the CSMM and Duplicate ICAO alert conditions and the escalation threshold.

The readback_check and spot_the_error activity types are well-suited to the phraseology content in 5-2-22 and 5-2-23. Ensure their explanations connect the correct answer to the operational trigger (malfunction jeopardizing ATC functions, not just any anomaly).

When generating items for 5-2-21 altitude filters, test the operational distinctions between general terminal positions, stratified positions, and positions responsible to the airport surface, rather than testing paragraph-level trivia.
Never embed paragraph numbers (e.g., 'under 5-2-24' or 'Per 5-2-24a') in stems or explanations; state the operational rule directly.
Balance distractor lengths within each multiple-choice question so the correct answer is not the uniquely longest option.
For exact-phraseology items, explanations must teach the operational context (when the phrase applies) rather than merely repeating the phrase.
Keep each flashcard to a single retrieval target per back; when a paragraph has multiple distinct controller duties, create separate cards.
Avoid generating activities that share identical stems and retrieval modes with existing questions; reinforce the same concept through a different activity type or angle instead.

When the source itself is a prohibition (e.g., 'Do not inform...'), a negative-stem question is appropriate; do not mechanically avoid negation when the skill being tested is recognition of a prohibited action.

Generate items for the 7700 display labels (ERAM: EMRG, STARS/MEARTS: EM) from 5-2-3 note seq 2. Use the 7600 display label items as a model but with substantive explanations that connect each label to the required 7700 emergency response.
When a paragraph has limited substance (5-2-4 has only three operational facts), cap item generation at one item per fact per format to avoid redundancy. 15 items for three facts is excessive.
For true/false items, the explanation must teach what makes the statement operationally correct, not merely identify which note or paragraph it comes from.
Flashcard backs must state the complete fact, not a bare abbreviation: 'ERAM displays RDOF instead of 7600' is self-contained; 'RDOF.' is not.

The 5-2-3 flashcards demonstrate good practice: self-contained fronts and backs, appropriate card types matched to content (phraseology for exact wording, exception for conditional rules, requirement for mandatory actions).

Scale target counts to source substance density. A paragraph with three short blocks cannot support five questions, five activities, and five flashcards without redundancy. For paragraphs this thin, reduce each format's target count or merge adjacent paragraphs into composite coverage.

Combine system-variant information into single items. The ERAM/STARS note should produce one item per format (e.g., one question asking for both display labels, one flashcard covering both) rather than one per system per format.

When source blocks contain only directives and labels with no rationale, accept operational explanations that state the rule and its conditions. Do not force causal or safety rationales the source does not provide.

Reserve true/false and requirement_check formats for binary discrimination between closely related concepts (e.g., 7500 vs 7600 scope). Do not use them to restate a directive already tested by multiple-choice items.

Prefer situation_action and knowledge_check activities over requirement_check and scope_check when the source block describes an operational action, as they more naturally demand application and recognition.
For exception-type flashcards, require a back that states the distinction operationally, not a binary Yes/No that conveys no substance. The back must be a self-contained retrieval target.
Balance distractor specificity against correct-answer length. When the correct answer is a document name or compound list, make distractors similarly specific rather than short generic phrases.
For source statements that enumerate a minimum set of items, prefer an exclusion-recognition format (which is NOT required) over brute recall of five concatenated elements in a single choice.

Do not generate both a question and an activity for the same retrieval target unless the activity's operational format (situation_action, scenario_step, etc.) adds a meaningfully different retrieval mode. True/false capability_checks cloning true_false questions waste item slots.

When a source invokes a cross-reference as the controlling action (e.g., treat per 10-1-1c), the explanation must state that cross-reference and its significance rather than only describing the operational outcome.

Keep flashcard backs to one to three focused retrieval targets. Cardinals that are inherently thin (e.g., OS/CIC) should include the operational purpose stated in the source (so the event can be documented).
Flashcard card_type should match the content: 'definition' for term/concept pairs, 'requirement' for procedural duties, 'exception' for departures from the general rule.
Strip all paragraph-number references from stems, explanations, and para_context fields; the learner needs operational prompts, not document navigation.

Add coverage for 5-2-7 VFR transponder exceptions: gliders squawk 1202, formation leads squawk 1203, SAR missions may squawk 1277, firefighting may squawk 1255. These are materially distinct retrieval targets.
Add coverage for 5-2-8 Note 2: the beacon code is often preset on the ground for pressure-suit/FL 600 flights and crew may be unable to accept transponder changes due to equipment inaccessibility.
For 5-2-8 and 5-2-9, reinforce the controller's discretion ('if necessary', 'may request', 'as appropriate') in code-change scenarios; the language is permissive, not mandatory.
Flashcard backs must answer only the question posed on the front; do not append unrelated adjacent facts.
Knowledge-check explanations must teach the controlling operational principle, not merely restate the correct answer in shorter form.
Avoid long correct-answer choices for multiple-choice questions when the same rule can be stated more concisely without distorting the controlling conditions.

Write every stem without paragraph numbers. A stem must be operationally self-contained — the controller should recognize the situation and rule without knowing which section of the .65 it appears in.

Explain the rule, not the location. Replace 'Per 5-3-1, the controller must…' with the operational statement directly: 'Radar identification must be established and maintained before providing radar service, except for the listed exceptions.'

Match distractor length and specificity to the correct answer. When the correct answer requires detail (e.g., listing two conditions for departure identification), make distractors similarly specific to avoid length cues.

Never write items where the correct answer is a paragraph number, a subparagraph reference, or any document-structure element. Test the operational substance of exceptions, not where they are documented.

For multi-condition requirements (e.g., identifying turns has three conditions), phrase the stem to acknowledge there are multiple conditions and ask about one of them — 'Which of the following is a required condition…' rather than 'What condition must be met…'

When an item references exceptions, consider whether the learner needs to know what the exceptions are, not just that they exist. The 5-5-1(b2,b3) and 8-5-5 exceptions are substantive and would benefit from dedicated retrieval items covering their operational content.
For knowledge_check activities that test the same concept as a multiple-choice question, vary the specific method, distractor angle, or difficulty to justify the duplication across entity types.

Strip every paragraph reference from stems, explanations, and activity context fields. Cite the rule, not its location. For example, 'Per 5-3-4c' must become the operational statement 'A displaced data block must be updated at all times.'

Explanations must teach the controlling principle, not restate the paragraph number. When the source states only a prescriptive requirement without elaboration, explain the requirement's scope and application — but do not invent safety rationales, system-design purposes, or operational consequences absent from the source.

Match distractor length and specificity to the correct answer. When a compound source rule makes the correct answer necessarily longer, expand distractors with realistic operational detail rather than leaving them as terse one-liners.

One retrieval target per flashcard. When paragraphs contain related but distinct rules (e.g., 5-3-5a on multiple identification methods and 5-3-5b on immediate re-identification), create separate cards for each.

Ensure each source block has balanced cross-format coverage. 5-3-5b2 (en route primary target display) is underrepresented — it has only one true/false item and no activity or flashcard reinforcement.

Negative stems for exclusion recognition are appropriate when testing knowledge of exceptions (e.g., when position information is NOT required), but the stem should frame the operational decision, not a paragraph-hunting exercise.

Activity types should match the skill: knowledge_check for operational decisions, conditional_rule_check for verifying rule application, list_membership for code/symbol recognition, situation_action for applying rules in context. The current type assignments are appropriate.

Strip every paragraph reference from stems, explanations, and activity context fields. Replace 'Per 5-3-7b' with the operational statement: 'When radar contact is lost, inform the aircraft with RADAR CONTACT LOST and provide alternative instructions when required.'

Do not duplicate a retrieval target across formats that test the same skill in the same mode. The en route and terminal data block retention rules are identical — generate one question, not two. The knowledge_check on minimum data block display should not duplicate a multiple_choice question on the same fact.

One retrieval target per flashcard. When a paragraph contains distinct rules (retention, display minimums, ERAM-specific requirement), create separate cards. The back should be concise enough for a single retrieval event.

Match distractor specificity and length to the correct answer. When a correct answer necessarily quotes a compound source rule, expand distractors with realistic operational detail rather than one-liners.
Use only standard FAA phraseology in all answer choices and distractors. Nonstandard terms like 'NO JOY' have no place in ATC instructional material.

Activity type assignments are appropriate: readback_check for phraseology verification, situation_action for applying rules in context, knowledge_check for operational decision recall. Maintain these type-to-skill mappings.

Explanations must teach the controlling operational principle directly. When the source states only a prescriptive duty without elaboration, explain the scope of application. Do not invent safety rationales or system-design purposes absent from the source.
Never embed paragraph-location references in stems or explanations. Write prompts that are self-contained operational questions.
Explanations must teach the controlling principle, condition, or exception from the source — not restate the answer, not cite paragraph numbers, and not invent unsupported safety rationales.
When writing definitions from source blocks, include all distinguishing elements (e.g., 'or the appropriate automated system response' in Point Out Approved).
Cross-format items should reinforce the same concept through different retrieval demands, not duplicate the same stem and choices.
Match distractor length and specificity to the correct answer to avoid answer-length cues.
For phraseology-builder explanations, teach the format template (e.g., 'POINT OUT [position], [aircraft ID], [altitude]') rather than repeating the assembled target phrase.
Never include paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or activity context fields. Write self-contained items that test the operational rule, not the student's ability to navigate the document.

Write explanations that teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception using at least 10 words. An explanation should convey why the answer is correct in operational terms, not merely restate the answer or cite a paragraph identifier.

Ensure correct-answer and distractor choices have comparable length. When the correct answer requires a compound condition with an exception, either balance it by adding context to distractors or teach the exception in the explanation and keep the choice text focused.

When placing the same source statement in question and activity formats, vary the operational angle, tested skill, or situational context to achieve genuine cross-format reinforcement rather than copy-paste duplication.

Target flashcard backs to one procedure, checklist, or distinct concept. Avoid compressing duties from multiple subparagraphs into a single back unless the complete set is the required retrieval target.

Review uncovered source material for coverage in subsequent generation passes. In this shard, 5-4-5 subparagraphs c, e, h3-h4, j, and k and 5-4-6 subparagraphs b and d have no corresponding learning items.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, prompts, explanations, or para_context fields; items must be self-contained around the operational rule.

Do not produce knowledge_check or requirement_check activities that duplicate the concept and retrieval mode of an existing MC or T/F question. Use these activity types only when the retrieval task differs from the question pool.

When testing conjunctive conditions (e.g., three requirements for AIT without verbal coordination), do not make the correct answer a compound sentence while distractors are single clauses — balance answer length or use a scenario-based format.
Flashcard backs must derive entirely from the source blocks assigned to that paragraph; do not pull content from adjacent paragraphs without verifying it belongs to the assigned source.
Explanations must state the controlling operational principle (what the rule requires, when it applies, what the exceptions are) rather than citing a paragraph number or restating the answer.

For this chapter section (coordination methods), prefer situation_action activities that require applying the rule to a specific operational scenario over generic knowledge_check items that only test recall of conditions.
Ensure the receiving-controller responsibilities in 5-4-7 (target correspondence, separation, issuing restrictions) receive coverage beyond a single scope_check T/F activity.

Strip all paragraph-number citations from stems, explanations, para_context fields, and activity instructions. Students must learn to recognize when a rule applies from the operational situation, not from a paragraph label.
Write explanations that teach the controlling condition or exception. When a rule states a duty or threshold without offering a reason, explain the duty directly — do not invent safety rationales.

When a paragraph contains multiple sub-provisions (e.g., 5-5-1b3's five cumulative conditions), distribute coverage across them rather than drilling one sub-provision repeatedly. A single 5-5-1 paragraph can support four to six distinct retrieval targets.
Avoid creating table_lookup or reference-identification items that ask which table or paragraph is referenced. Instead, use the referenced table as source material for operational lookup decisions.
For knowledge_check activities, avoid generic references like 'under this section' or 'the application paragraph.' State the operational condition explicitly.
Match flashcard card_type to content: a card stating operational minima is a 'procedure,' not a 'definition.'
Strip all paragraph-location references ('under 5-5-X', 'Per 5-5-X') from stems, explanations, and para_context fields. Replace with operational condition descriptions from the source blocks.

Do not generate true/false items that simply restate a rule already covered by a multiple-choice question. Reserve T/F for distinct operational boundaries, exceptions, or common misconceptions not addressed by the MCQ.

Generate items for the three target resolution application sub-rules in 5-5-3 c1-c3: between edges of two primary targets, between end of beacon slash and edge of primary target, between ends of two beacon control slashes. These are currently untested.
Add a dedicated flashcard or question for 5-5-2 sub-rule c (end of beacon control slash to center of primary target), currently carried only by the overloaded combined flashcard.

When writing explanations, state the controlling operational condition and the required action together. Do not merely echo the chosen answer. The source provides the context for when and why a rule applies; include that context.

Keep explanations factual and grounded in the source blocks. Do not invent safety rationales, system-design claims, or operational consequences. If the source only states a duty or threshold, explain that duty or threshold directly.
Strip all paragraph references from stems, explanations, and activity context fields. State operational rules without citing paragraph numbers.
Avoid true/false questions that restate source text verbatim. When T/F is appropriate, test a specific operational condition or edge case that requires applying the rule, not recognizing it.
Ensure multiple-choice distractors are comparable in length and specificity to the correct answer. Trimming correct-answer wording or expanding plausible distractors both work.
For flashcards, include the operational condition or timing in the back text (e.g., not just the spacing value but when it applies). Avoid vague closure like 'under the paragraph's standards.'
When a source paragraph prescribes multiple methods, either ask about a specific method or ensure all valid methods are represented or clearly excluded.

For table_lookup activities, continue naming the source table — that is appropriate format scaffolding, not paragraph-location trivia. The distinction is that students need to know which table to reference during operations.

Generate items that test the en route passing/diverging procedure (5-5-7b): the five conditions (communications, traffic information, pilot report of sighting and passing, radar target observation, heavy/super advisory) and the operational sequence.

Cover nonstandard formation separation (5-5-8c): applying minima to the perimeter of the airspace encompassing the formation or from the outermost aircraft. This sub-rule has zero items in the current shard.
Add obstruction-separation items for single-sensor MSSR (5-5-9a3: 3 miles when less than 60 miles from antenna) and STARS Multi-Sensor Mode (5-5-9a5: 5 miles).
When generating new phraseology items, ensure explanations teach when the instruction is issued and what prompts it — not just that the phrase is correct.
Avoid embedding paragraph numbers in stems. Use operational descriptions: sensor mode, facility type, distance from antenna, formation type — the conditions a controller actually sees.
Keep answer choices roughly similar in length and specificity. Long correct answers and short distractors create a presentation cue independent of ATC knowledge.

The 5-5-7a3/b6 wake-turbulence exception is tested by one true/false question. Consider a situation_action or knowledge_check that embeds this in an operational context requiring the controller to recognize when wake turbulence overrides passing/diverging.
Flashcard backs should cite specific minima rather than placeholder phrases like 'the required angle' or 'the required spacing.'
Remove all paragraph-location references from stems and explanations; operational framing is sufficient — the source is the rulebook, not a lookup target.
Balance multiple-choice option lengths so the correct answer does not stand out by length; each distractor should be comparably specific and detailed.
For true/false items, avoid drafting the stem as a verbatim source quotation; test whether the controller can identify the rule's conditions, not whether they can match text.
Explanations must teach the controlling principle, condition, or operational consequence stated in the source — not merely name the paragraph or restate the answer.
Flashcard backs must be self-contained retrieval targets; avoid phrases like 'as described in the paragraph' or 'in facility directives' without stating the actual rule.
Continue generating situation_action activities with realistic operational dilemmas; the 5-6-1 and 5-6-3 situation items test decision-making effectively once scaffolding is stripped.
When a single paragraph contains multiple distinct rules (e.g., 5-6-3's three obstacle-separation conditions), ensure items distribute across those rules rather than clustering on one.
Do not convert well-formed phraseology_builder, spot_the_error, or readback_check activities that test exact transmission language into generic multiple-choice questions.

When the source block includes a note that cancels or transforms a procedure (ODP cancellation after vector, SID/STAR restriction cancellation), generate at least one item that tests the consequence, not just the trigger condition.
Strip every paragraph citation from stems, explanations, and activity context fields. Design items so the student never needs to know a paragraph number.

Write explanations that state the operational rule, its conditions, and its application. An explanation that says 'Per 5-7-2b' teaches nothing; one that describes the FL390 concurrence requirement and the pilot's prerogative teaches the content.
Match distractor length and syntactic complexity to the correct answer. When the correct answer bundles three principles into a compound sentence, write distractors of comparable scope and length.
Do not test the same operational fact in two different multiple-choice-style items. Cross-format reinforcement is fine, but two MCQ-style items on FL390 pilot consent add no retrieval diversity.

Use the approved instruction forms from 5-7-2a as the basis for distractor construction in knowledge checks — non-prescribed verbs and unspecific phrasing make plausible, educationally valuable wrong answers.
Scenario-based activities are the strongest items in this shard. The next generation pass should invest in more operational vignettes and fewer isolated fact-recall items.

Strip every paragraph citation from stems, explanations, and activity context fields. Design items so the student never needs to know a paragraph number to answer the question or understand the explanation.

Write explanations that state the operational rule, its conditions, and its application. An explanation that says 'Per 5-7-3d1' teaches nothing; one that states 'Turbojet departures must be assigned a speed not less than 230 knots' teaches the content.

Match distractor length and syntactic complexity to the correct answer. When the correct answer is a multi-clause sentence, write distractors of comparable scope rather than short declarative statements.

Do not add qualifiers to flashcard fronts that narrow the scope beyond what the source states. The 5-7-3b rule applies to all aircraft beneath Class B or in VFR corridors — the word 'arrival' was an unsupported addition.
Do not pack multiple distinct rules into a single flashcard back. Each card should target one retrieval concept. If two rules are independently testable, they belong on two cards.

Scenario-based activities are the strongest items in this shard. Future generation passes should invest more in operational vignettes that require applying speed assignment rules to specific aircraft types and flight phases.
Never open a stem with 'Under 5-X-Y' or embed paragraph numbers in any item text visible to the learner. Frame the question around the operational situation the rule governs.
