operations.problem: The front asks what code to use when radar advisory service ends, but the back answers with two distinct facts: the termination code (squawk VFR/1200) and the active-service code (computer-assigned beacon code). The second sentence is unrelated to the front's question, overloading the retrieval target.
operations.source_basis: Instruct a VFR aircraft for which radar advisory service is being terminated to squawk VFR. The published phraseology is SQUAWK VFR or SQUAWK 1200.

match.front: What code should a VFR aircraft use when radar advisory service ends?
match.back: Instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR or 1200. VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories should have a computer-assigned beacon code while the service is being provided.

replacements.front: What code should a VFR aircraft use when radar advisory service ends?
replacements.back: Instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR or 1200.

operations.problem: The stem asks 'under 5-2-8' and the explanation quotes 'Per 5-2-8a', converting an operational procedure question into paragraph-location recall. The learner needs to know the normal code rule for pressure-suit and high-altitude flights, not its location in the order.
operations.source_basis: Ensure that these flights remain on one of the special use codes if filed in the flight plan, except when unforeseen events cause same-code conflicts.

match.question_text: What is the normal beacon-code rule under 5-2-8 for pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600 when a special-use code is filed?

replacements.question_text: What is the normal beacon-code rule for pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600 when a special-use code is filed?
replacements.explanation: Ensure these flights remain on one of the special-use codes if filed in the flight plan, except when unforeseen same-code conflicts require intervention.

choices.text: Ensure the flight remains on one of the filed special-use codes.
Always assign an ordinary discrete ARTCC code after takeoff.
Tell the pilot to squawk VFR below FL 600 and resume the special-use code only above FL 600.
Use Code 4000 for all pressure suit flights regardless of what is filed.

operations.problem: The stem ends with 'under 5-2-8' and the explanation quotes 'Per 5-2-8b', adding paragraph-location scaffolding. Additionally, the correct answer choice is substantially longer than all distractors, creating an answer-length cue that reveals the correct response without operational reasoning.
operations.source_basis: When unforeseen events cause more than one aircraft to be in the same or adjacent ARTCC's airspace at the same time on the same special use discrete code, if necessary, you may request the pilot to make a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate.

match.question_text: When may a controller request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk under 5-2-8?

replacements.question_text: When may a controller request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk for pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600?
replacements.explanation: If unforeseen events place more than one aircraft in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time on the same special-use discrete code, the controller may request a code change, standby, or stop squawk as appropriate, if necessary.

choices.text: When multiple aircraft on the same special-use code unexpectedly occupy the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace, and action is necessary.
Whenever a pilot asks for a more convenient code, regardless of mission status.
Only when the aircraft descends below FL 600.
Whenever automation shows multiple targets on one code, because a code change is always mandatory.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 5-2-8' and the explanation includes 'note 3 in 5-2-8'. The true/false statement about emergency code display labels is factually correct but the paragraph-location scaffolding teaches document navigation instead of the operational fact that Code 7700 remains available and is displayed differently by ERAM versus STARS/MEARTS.
operations.source_basis: In case of emergency, Code 7700 can still be activated. Instead of displaying '7700' in the data block, ERAM will display 'EMRG,' and STARS/MEARTS will display 'EM.'

match.question_text: Under 5-2-8, in an emergency Code 7700 can still be activated, and ERAM may display EMRG while STARS or MEARTS may display EM.

replacements.question_text: For pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600, in an emergency Code 7700 can still be activated, and ERAM may display EMRG while STARS or MEARTS may display EM.
replacements.explanation: Code 7700 remains available for emergency use on these flights. Instead of displaying '7700' in the data block, ERAM displays 'EMRG' and STARS/MEARTS displays 'EM.'

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The para_context opens with 'Under 5-2-8' and the explanation begins with 'Per 5-2-8', adding paragraph-location scaffolding. The situation-action scenario is well-constructed and tests the controller's discretion to resolve same-code conflicts for pressure-suit flights.
operations.source_basis: When unforeseen events cause more than one aircraft to be in the same or adjacent ARTCC's airspace at the same time on the same special use discrete code, if necessary, you may request the pilot to make a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate.

content.situation: Two pressure suit flights unexpectedly end up in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time while using the same special-use discrete beacon code, and the duplicate code is beginning to create an operational problem.
content.para_context: Under 5-2-8, pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600 normally remain on the filed special-use code. If unforeseen events place more than one aircraft in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time on the same special-use discrete code, you may request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate.

choices.text: If necessary, request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate to resolve the special-use code conflict.
Take no action because special-use flights must remain on the same code under all circumstances.
Assign Code 1200 because all special-use code conflicts are resolved with standard VFR coding.
Cancel the flights' special handling and issue ordinary terminal discrete codes regardless of mission limitations.

content.explanation: Per 5-2-8, special-use flights normally remain on the filed code, but an unforeseen same-code conflict may be resolved by requesting a code change, standby, or stop squawk when necessary.

content.situation: Two pressure suit flights unexpectedly end up in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time while using the same special-use discrete beacon code, and the duplicate code is beginning to create an operational problem.
content.para_context: Pressure suit flights and flights above FL 600 normally remain on the filed special-use code. If unforeseen events place more than one aircraft in the same or adjacent ARTCC airspace at the same time on the same special-use discrete code, you may request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate.

choices.text: If necessary, request a code change, squawk standby, or stop squawk as appropriate to resolve the special-use code conflict.
Take no action because special-use flights must remain on the same code under all circumstances.
Assign Code 1200 because all special-use code conflicts are resolved with standard VFR coding.
Cancel the flights' special handling and issue ordinary terminal discrete codes regardless of mission limitations.

operations.problem: This question asks which paragraph numbers are cited as exceptions to the radar identification rule. The correct answer consists entirely of paragraph references ('Paragraph 5-5-1, subparagraphs b2 and b3, and paragraph 8-5-5'). Teaching controllers to memorize paragraph numbers is educationally harmful — the operational skill is knowing what the exceptions are, not where they are documented in the .65.
operations.source_basis: The source block cross-references 'paragraph 5-5-1, Application, subparagraphs b2, b3 and paragraph 8-5-5, Radar Identification Application' as exceptions. These are document-structure references; the substantive operational content of the exceptions resides in those referenced paragraphs, not in the reference numbers themselves. The controller needs to know that specific cases in 5-5-1(b2,b3) and 8-5-5 permit radar service without prior identification — the paragraph numbers are an index, not a retrievable procedure.

operations.problem: Stem references the paragraph number and asks 'what condition' in the singular, implying only one condition exists for identifying turns. The source prescribes three distinct conditions. The item should be self-contained and acknowledge that multiple conditions apply.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-2c requires three conditions for identifying turns: (1) a pilot position report assuring the aircraft is within radar coverage and displayed area (except for lost aircraft), (2) only one aircraft is observed making these turns, and (3) for IFR aircraft, either issue a heading away from an area requiring increased minimum IFR altitude or have the aircraft climb to the highest minimum altitude in the controller's jurisdiction before issuing a heading.

match.question_text: What condition must be met before using identifying turns under 5-3-2?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is a required condition when using an identifying turn of 30 degrees or more for primary radar identification?
replacements.explanation: One required condition is that only one aircraft is observed making the identifying turn. The other conditions are: a pilot position report assuring the aircraft is within radar coverage and displayed area (except for lost aircraft), and for IFR aircraft, issuing a heading away from areas requiring increased minimum IFR altitude or having the aircraft climb to the highest minimum altitude in the controller's jurisdiction before issuing a heading.

choices.text: Only one aircraft is observed making the identifying turn or turns of 30 degrees or more.
Any number of aircraft may make similar turns as long as the controller suspects which one is correct.
The aircraft must be following a normal IFR route or known VFR flight path to reduce misidentification risk.
No pilot position report is needed before using identifying turns.

Only after the tower issues a landing clearance and the flight progress strip has been transferred.

content.explanation: Terminal automation identification requires both a visible data block and a qualifying confirmation condition: either the target identity has been confirmed by radar or beacon identification procedures, or the automated handoff status is valid without any disqualifying indicator such as CST, NAT, NT, AMB, OLD, AM, or TRK in the data block.

operations.problem: The explanation is a thin paragraph citation ('That requirement appears in 5-3-4c') that teaches nothing about the operational requirement. The question text and true/false structure are otherwise sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-4c: 'A displaced data block must be updated at all times.'

content.question_text: A displaced data block must be updated at all times.

False

False

content.explanation: A displaced data block must be updated at all times. This is an unconditional requirement — whenever the data block separates from the appropriate target for any reason, the controller must correct it immediately rather than rely on a displaced data block for target identity.

operations.problem: The para_context field opens with 'Under 5-3-6' and embeds 'in 5-3-3', converting what should be operational context into paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation also cites the paragraph number rather than teaching the rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-6: 'Inform an aircraft of its position whenever radar identification is established by means of identifying turns or by any of the beacon identification methods. content.situation: You identify an aircraft by a beacon identification method under 5-3-3 rather than by position correlation. The aircraft was not a departure identified within one mile of the runway end.
content.para_context: Under 5-3-6, inform an aircraft of its position whenever radar identification is established by identifying turns or by any of the beacon identification methods in 5-3-3. Position information is not required when identification is established by position correlation or when a departing aircraft is identified within one mile of the takeoff runway end.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft of its position because identification was established by a beacon identification method.
Do not provide position information because it is required only after position correlation.
Wait until the aircraft requests a position report because position information is never issued automatically.
Withhold position information unless the aircraft is on an IFR flight plan.

content.explanation: Per 5-3-6, position information is given when identification is established by identifying turns or beacon identification methods, except for the listed exceptions.

content.situation: You identify an aircraft by a beacon identification method rather than by position correlation. The aircraft was not a departure identified within one mile of the runway end.
content.para_context: Inform an aircraft of its position whenever radar identification is established by identifying turns or by any beacon identification method. Position information is not required when identification is established by position correlation or when a departing aircraft is identified within one mile of the takeoff runway end.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft of its position because identification was established by a beacon identification method.
Do not provide position information because it is required only after position correlation.
Wait until the aircraft requests a position report because position information is never issued automatically.
Withhold position information unless the aircraft is on an IFR flight plan.

content.explanation: Position information is required when identification was established by a beacon identification method. The situation specifically rules out both exceptions: the identification was not by position correlation, and the aircraft was not a departure within one mile of the runway end. Position information must be provided.

operations.problem: The front asks what to do when target identification is questionable, but the back opens with a different rule ('Use more than one identification method when doubt exists') from 5-3-5a before eventually answering the front's question with the 5-3-5b rule. This overloads a single card with two distinct retrieval targets from different sub-paragraphs.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-5b: 'If identification is questionable for any reason, take immediate action to re-identify the aircraft or terminate radar service.'

match.front: What must you do if target identification is questionable?
match.back: Use more than one identification method when doubt exists. If identification is questionable, immediately reidentify the aircraft or terminate radar service.

replacements.front: What must you do if target identification is questionable?
replacements.back: Take immediate action to re-identify the aircraft or terminate radar service.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-3-7'). The distractor 'NO JOY, recycle transponder now' uses nonstandard, unprofessional language that is not FAA phraseology. The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-7b' instead of teaching the phraseology rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-7 phraseology block: 'RADAR CONTACT LOST (alternative instructions when required).'

replacements.question_text: What phraseology is used to inform an aircraft that radar contact has been lost?
replacements.explanation: When radar contact is lost, the controller must inform the aircraft using the phrase 'RADAR CONTACT LOST,' and must append alternative instructions when the situation requires them — for example, a heading or altitude to maintain while contact is reestablished.

choices.text: RADAR CONTACT LOST, with alternative instructions when required.
RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED, squawk VFR.
RADAR IDENTIFICATION LOST, recycle transponder.
TRAFFIC NO FACTOR, resume own navigation.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-3-7'). The correct answer is substantially longer than the distractors, creating a reliable length cue. The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-7a' instead of the operational rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-7a: Inform an aircraft of radar contact when initial radar identification in the ATC system is established, and when radar identification is reestablished after loss of radar contact or termination of radar service.

match.question_text: When must a controller advise an aircraft of radar contact under 5-3-7?

replacements.question_text: When must a controller inform an aircraft of radar contact?
replacements.explanation: Radar contact must be announced in two circumstances: when initial radar identification is first established in the ATC system, and when radar identification is reestablished after it was previously lost or after radar service had been terminated. Both events require the controller to issue the advisory.

choices.text: When initial radar identification is established, and also when it is reestablished after loss or termination of radar service.
Only on the first radar contact of the flight, regardless of whether contact is later lost and reestablished.
Only when the pilot specifically asks whether radar contact exists.
Only immediately before radar service is about to be terminated.

operations.problem: The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-7a2' — a paragraph-location reference that adds no operational understanding. The stem and answer are otherwise sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-7a2: Subsequent to loss of radar contact or terminating radar service, radar identification is reestablished — inform the aircraft of radar contact.

match.question_text: If radar contact is reestablished after being lost, the controller advises the aircraft of radar contact again.

replacements.question_text: If radar contact is reestablished after being lost, the controller advises the aircraft of radar contact again.
replacements.explanation: When radar identification is reestablished after a loss of radar contact or after radar service was terminated, the controller must again advise the aircraft of radar contact. The advisory is not a one-time event — it applies each time identification is established or reestablished.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-7' as a bare paragraph reference instead of teaching the readback principle. The activity content itself is well-constructed.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-7 phraseology: 'RADAR CONTACT LOST (alternative instructions when required).'

content.instruction: The controller advised the aircraft that radar contact was lost and issued an alternate instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
content.clearance: RADAR CONTACT LOST. FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO.

choices.text: Radar contact lost, fly heading zero niner zero, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar contact, fly heading zero niner zero, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar service terminated, squawk VFR, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar contact lost, ident, Learjet Four Six Golf.

content.explanation: Per 5-3-7, when radar contact is lost the aircraft is informed with 'RADAR CONTACT LOST' plus alternative instructions when required.

content.instruction: The controller advised the aircraft that radar contact was lost and issued an alternate instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?
content.clearance: RADAR CONTACT LOST. FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO.

choices.text: Radar contact lost, fly heading zero niner zero, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar contact, fly heading zero niner zero, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar service terminated, squawk VFR, Learjet Four Six Golf.
Radar contact lost, ident, Learjet Four Six Golf.

content.explanation: When radar contact is lost, the controller issues 'RADAR CONTACT LOST' with any required alternative instructions. The pilot must read back both the loss-of-contact advisory and the alternative instruction verbatim. Omitting 'LOST' or substituting a different action changes the meaning of the transmission.

operations.problem: The para_context field leads with 'Under 5-3-7' — paragraph-location scaffolding that distracts from the operational content. The choices and explanation are otherwise sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-7a: Inform an aircraft of radar contact when initial radar identification is established and when radar identification is reestablished after loss of radar contact or termination of radar service.

content.situation: Radar identification has just been reestablished on an aircraft after radar contact was previously lost.
content.para_context: Under 5-3-7, inform an aircraft of radar contact when initial radar identification is established and when radar identification is reestablished after loss of radar contact or termination of radar service. Inform the aircraft when radar contact is lost.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft of radar contact again because radar identification has been reestablished.
Do not say anything because radar contact is announced only once per flight.
Wait until the next frequency change before advising radar contact.
Terminate radar service immediately because reestablished contact cannot be used.

content.explanation: Per 5-3-7, radar contact is stated when it is initially established and again when it is reestablished after being lost or after radar service had been terminated.

content.situation: Radar identification has just been reestablished on an aircraft after radar contact was previously lost.
content.para_context: Inform an aircraft of radar contact when initial radar identification is established and when radar identification is reestablished after loss of radar contact or termination of radar service. Inform the aircraft when radar contact is lost.

choices.text: Advise the aircraft of radar contact again because radar identification has been reestablished.
Do not say anything because radar contact is announced only once per flight.
Wait until the next frequency change before advising radar contact.
Terminate radar service immediately because reestablished contact cannot be used.

content.explanation: Radar contact must be advised each time radar identification is established or reestablished. Loss of radar contact does not create a permanent exemption — once identification is restored, the advisory is required again.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-3-8'). The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-8' instead of teaching the retention requirement and its scope.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-8: Retain data blocks associated with the appropriate target symbol to maintain continuous identity of aircraft. Retain the data block until the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace, and all potential conflicts have been resolved; including an aircraft that is a point out.

match.question_text: How long is an en route data block retained under 5-3-8?

replacements.question_text: How long must an en route controller retain a data block associated with a target symbol?
replacements.explanation: An en route data block must be retained until two conditions are both met: the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace, and all potential conflicts have been resolved. This includes aircraft that are point-outs — they are not exempt from the retention requirement until their conflicts are cleared.

choices.text: Until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts are resolved, including point-outs.
Only until the aircraft is radar identified for the first time.
Only until the aircraft enters delegated airspace, even if conflicts remain unresolved.
Until the next controller verbally acknowledges seeing the target symbol.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-3-8'). The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-8' rather than describing what the minimum display requirement entails.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-8: The data block must display flight identification and altitude information, as a minimum. The displayed altitude may be assigned, interim, or reported.

match.question_text: What minimum information must an en route data block display under 5-3-8?

replacements.question_text: What minimum information must an en route data block display?
replacements.explanation: At a minimum, an en route data block must display two pieces of information: flight identification and altitude. The altitude value may be assigned, interim, or reported — any of these three altitude sources satisfies the minimum display requirement.

choices.text: Flight identification and altitude information, at a minimum.
Only a target symbol and ground speed readout.
Only flight identification if altitude information is available on an adjacent display.
Only the reported altitude, because the flight identification is retained separately.

operations.problem: The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-8' — a bare paragraph reference that does not teach the operational requirement behind the ERAM full data block rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-8 ERAM provision: When you have separation responsibility for an aircraft and a paired track exists, display a full data block (FDB).

match.question_text: In ERAM, when you have separation responsibility for an aircraft and a paired track exists, you display a full data block.

replacements.question_text: In ERAM, when you have separation responsibility for an aircraft and a paired track exists, you must display a full data block.
replacements.explanation: The ERAM system requires display of a full data block (FDB) when both conditions are met: the controller has separation responsibility for the aircraft, and a paired track exists. This is a mandatory display requirement, not discretionary.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity tests the same retrieval target as the multiple_choice question 14cc6ab4: the minimum information an en route data block must display. Retaining both creates redundant retrieval with no educational gain.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-8: The data block must display flight identification and altitude information, as a minimum. The displayed altitude may be assigned, interim, or reported.

Ground speed and destination airport only.

operations.problem: The para_context field leads with 'Under 5-3-8' — paragraph-location scaffolding. The activity content is otherwise well-constructed and tests the compound retention and ERAM requirements in an integrated operational scenario.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-8: Retain data blocks until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts are resolved, including point-outs. Data block must display flight identification and altitude minimum. ERAM: when separation responsibility exists and a paired track exists, display a full data block.

content.para_context: Under 5-3-8, retain data blocks associated with the correct target symbol to maintain continuous identity until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts are resolved, including point-outs. The data block must display flight identification and altitude information at a minimum. In ERAM, when you have separation responsibility and a paired track exists, display a full data block.

choices.text: Retain the associated data block and display a full data block because the aircraft is still within delegated airspace, potential conflicts remain, and a paired ERAM track exists.
Drop the data block now because point-outs are exempt from the retention requirement once the aircraft enters delegated airspace.
Use only the target symbol because flight identification and altitude information are optional after initial identification.
Suppress the paired track because ERAM full data blocks are not displayed when separation responsibility exists.

content.explanation: Per 5-3-8, retain the associated data block until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and conflicts are resolved, and display a full ERAM data block when a paired track exists and you have separation responsibility.

content.situation: You have separation responsibility for an en route aircraft, a paired track exists in ERAM, and the aircraft is still a point out with unresolved potential conflicts inside delegated airspace.
content.para_context: Retain data blocks associated with the correct target symbol to maintain continuous identity until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts are resolved, including point-outs. The data block must display flight identification and altitude information at a minimum. In ERAM, when you have separation responsibility and a paired track exists, display a full data block.

choices.text: Retain the associated data block and display a full data block because the aircraft is still within delegated airspace, potential conflicts remain, and a paired ERAM track exists.
Drop the data block now because point-outs are exempt from the retention requirement once the aircraft enters delegated airspace.
Use only the target symbol because flight identification and altitude information are optional after initial identification.
Suppress the paired track because ERAM full data blocks are not displayed when separation responsibility exists.

content.explanation: The data block must be retained until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts are resolved — point-out aircraft are explicitly included in this requirement. Additionally, the ERAM provision requires a full data block when separation responsibility exists and a paired track is present.

operations.problem: The flashcard overloads a single card with three distinct retrieval targets: retention duration (when to drop the block), minimum display content (what must be shown), and the ERAM full-data-block condition. The front asks for 'minimum requirements' but the back covers retention rules that are not minimum-display requirements. The back is too long for a single retrieval event and mixes unlike concepts.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-8 requires data block retention until sector exit and conflict resolution, minimum display of flight identification and altitude, and ERAM full data block display when separation responsibility exists with a paired track.

match.front: What are the minimum en route data-block requirements?
match.back: Retain the data block until the aircraft and conflicts are clear. It must at least show flight identification and altitude, and ERAM paired tracks need a full data block when you own separation.

replacements.front: How long must an en route data block be retained?
replacements.back: Until the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts have been resolved, including aircraft that are point-outs.

replacements.front: What minimum information must an en route data block display?
replacements.back: Flight identification and altitude information. The displayed altitude may be assigned, interim, or reported.

replacements.front: In ERAM, when must a full data block (FDB) be displayed?
replacements.back: When you have separation responsibility for an aircraft and a paired track exists.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-3-9'). The explanation cites 'Per 5-3-9b' instead of teaching the prearranged coordination display rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-9b: During prearranged coordination procedures, the controllers who penetrate another controller's airspace must display data block information of that controller's aircraft which must contain, at a minimum, the position symbol and altitude information.

match.question_text: During prearranged coordination under 5-3-9, what minimum information must be displayed for another controller's aircraft when penetrating that controller's airspace?

replacements.question_text: During prearranged coordination, what minimum information must a controller display when penetrating another controller's airspace?
replacements.explanation: When a controller penetrates another controller's airspace under prearranged coordination procedures, the penetrating controller must display data block information for the other controller's aircraft. At a minimum, this display must contain the position symbol and altitude information. Note that this differs from the general data block requirement — it does not require flight identification.

choices.text: At least the position symbol and altitude information.
Only flight identification, matching the general data block minimum.
Only a conflict alert indicator if traffic is present.
No additional display is required because the coordination is prearranged.

operations.problem: This question tests the same data block retention rule as question 57b8b1b0 for 5-3-8. The retention requirement is word-for-word identical across the en route and terminal paragraphs: 'Retain the data block until the aircraft has exited the sector or delegated airspace, and all potential conflicts have been resolved; including an aircraft that is a point out.' The terminal-specific nuances (VFR exception in delegated airspace) are covered by question 48794563 and activity c5ab8ac3. This item adds no new retrieval target.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-9a repeats the identical retention rule from 5-3-8: retain data blocks until the aircraft exits the sector or delegated airspace and all potential conflicts are resolved, including point-outs.

match.question_text: How long are terminal data blocks retained under 5-3-9?

operations.problem: The stem uses 'clear of that airspace' where 'that' is ambiguous — it could refer to the delegated airspace extending beyond Class B/C rather than specifically Class B and Class C airspace as the source states. The source requires the aircraft to be clear of Class B and Class C airspace specifically, not the broader delegated airspace. The explanation cites 'the note in 5-3-9' instead of directly teaching the exception.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-9 note: Where delegated airspace extends beyond Class B and/or Class C airspace, the following will apply: If a VFR aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated then retention of the data block is no longer required.

match.question_text: Where delegated airspace extends beyond Class B and or Class C airspace, retention of the data block is no longer required for a VFR aircraft once it is clear of that airspace and radar services have been terminated.

replacements.question_text: Where delegated airspace extends beyond Class B or Class C airspace, retention of a VFR aircraft's data block is no longer required once the aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated.
replacements.explanation: The terminal data block retention rule has a specific exception: when delegated airspace extends beyond Class B or Class C airspace, a VFR aircraft's data block may be dropped if the aircraft is clear of both Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated. The aircraft must clear both types of airspace — being clear of delegated airspace generally is not sufficient.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The correct answer is substantially longer than the distractors because it must state both required conditions (clear of Class B/C airspace and radar services terminated), creating a length cue. The explanation is thin — it says 'the note allows' without specifying the source note's conditions or teaching why the exception applies.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-9 note: Where delegated airspace extends beyond Class B and/or Class C airspace, if a VFR aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated then retention of the data block is no longer required.

content.question_text: For terminal operations, when is retention of a VFR aircraft's data block no longer required in delegated airspace extending beyond Class B or Class C?

choices.text: When the aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated.
Only after the aircraft lands at a towered airport.
As soon as the aircraft leaves the final approach area.
When the aircraft squawks standby.

content.explanation: The note allows the terminal data block to be dropped after the aircraft is clear of the relevant airspace and radar service has ended.

content.question_text: For terminal operations, when is retention of a VFR aircraft's data block no longer required in delegated airspace extending beyond Class B or Class C?

choices.text: When the aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated.
Only after the aircraft has landed at a towered airport within the delegated airspace.
As soon as the aircraft passes the final approach fix, regardless of radar service status.
When the aircraft squawks standby and the controller visually acquires it.

content.explanation: The data block retention requirement has a limited exception for VFR aircraft in terminal delegated airspace that extends beyond Class B or C. Both conditions must be satisfied: the aircraft must be clear of Class B and Class C airspace, and radar services must have been terminated. If either condition is unmet — the aircraft remains in Class B or C, or radar service continues — the data block must be retained.

operations.problem: The back imprecisely states 'aircraft is clear' without specifying what airspace it must be clear of. The source note requires the aircraft to be clear of Class B and Class C airspace specifically — omitting this loses the critical spatial condition. The front asks 'When may a terminal data block be dropped' but the answer leads with the general retention rule instead of directly answering the question.
operations.source_basis: 5-3-9: Retain terminal data blocks until aircraft exits sector or delegated airspace and conflicts resolve. Note exception: if delegated airspace extends beyond Class B/C, VFR block may be dropped when aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services terminated.

match.front: When may a terminal data block be dropped?
match.back: Retain it until the aircraft and conflicts are clear. If delegated airspace extends beyond Class B or C, a VFR block may be dropped once the aircraft is clear and radar service has ended.

replacements.front: When may a terminal data block for a VFR aircraft be dropped early?
replacements.back: When delegated airspace extends beyond Class B or C airspace, a VFR aircraft's data block may be dropped once the aircraft is clear of Class B and Class C airspace and radar services have been terminated.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('5-4-1'). The explanation cites 'Per 5-4-1' instead of teaching the transfer requirement and its operational scope.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-1: Interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where it is not operationally feasible.

match.question_text: What does 5-4-1 require regarding interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification?

replacements.question_text: Where must interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification be accomplished?
replacements.explanation: Transfers of radar identification — both between facilities and within the same facility — must be accomplished in every area of radar surveillance. The only exception is where it is not operationally feasible, and even then the constraint must be formally addressed through a letter of agreement or controller coordination.

choices.text: In all areas of radar surveillance, except where not operationally feasible.
Only in areas where both controllers can simultaneously view the target on their scopes.
Only for interfacility transfers; intrafacility transfers are optional.
Only when communications are transferred before the sector boundary crossing.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-4-1') and a negative framing ('not operationally feasible'). The correct answer is substantially longer than the distractors. The explanation cites 'Per 5-4-1' instead of teaching the two mandated remedies.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-1: Where transfer of radar identification is not operationally feasible, constraints must be covered in letters of agreement which clearly state that control will not be based upon a radar handoff, or coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time.

match.question_text: When radar handoff-based control is not operationally feasible under 5-4-1, how must that constraint be handled?

replacements.question_text: In areas where transfer of radar identification is not operationally feasible, how must that constraint be formally handled?
replacements.explanation: When transfer of radar identification is not operationally feasible in an area of radar surveillance, two formal remedies are authorized: the constraint must be covered in a letter of agreement clearly stating that control will not be based on a radar handoff, or the transferring and receiving controllers must coordinate the exception for a specified period of time. Informal practice or unilateral decisions are not acceptable substitutes.

choices.text: Cover it in a letter of agreement stating control is not based on a radar handoff, or coordinate it between the controllers for a specified period.
Rely on informal practice at the position without written or verbal coordination.
Transfer communications first and determine control responsibility after the fact.
Suspend all radar service in the affected airspace until the constraint is resolved.

operations.problem: The stem uses 'radar-handoff-based control' which is not the source language — the source discusses transfer of radar identification not being operationally feasible. The explanation cites 'Per 5-4-1' as a bare paragraph reference.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-1: Where transfer of radar identification is not operationally feasible, constraints must be covered in an LOA or coordinated by the controllers for a specified period of time.

match.question_text: If radar-handoff-based control is not operationally feasible, controllers may omit the transfer of radar identification without an LOA or specific coordination.

replacements.question_text: If transfer of radar identification is not operationally feasible, the controllers may handle the situation without a letter of agreement or specific coordination.
replacements.explanation: This statement is false. When transfer of radar identification is not operationally feasible, the constraint must be formally addressed — either through a letter of agreement stating that control will not be based on a radar handoff, or through coordination between the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time. The situation cannot be handled informally.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The explanation is thin — it says 'the workaround must be explicitly handled by LOA or direct coordination' without citing the source requirement or explaining why formal handling is required. The stem and choices are otherwise sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-1: Where transfers are not operationally feasible, constraints must be covered in an LOA clearly stating control will not be based on a radar handoff, or coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period.

content.question_text: If interfacility or intrafacility radar identification transfers are not operationally feasible, how must that limitation be handled?

choices.text: It must be covered in an LOA or coordinated by the controllers for a specified period of time.
It may be ignored if both controllers can see the same target.
It requires all aircraft to revert immediately to nonradar operations facility-wide.
It requires the receiving controller to accept all entries without handoff.

content.explanation: When routine transfer is not feasible, the workaround must be explicitly handled by LOA or direct coordination.

content.question_text: If interfacility or intrafacility radar identification transfers are not operationally feasible, how must that limitation be handled?

choices.text: It must be covered in an LOA or coordinated by the controllers for a specified period of time.
It may be ignored if both controllers can see the same target on their displays.
It requires all aircraft to revert immediately to nonradar operations facility-wide.
It requires the receiving controller to accept all entries without any handoff procedure.

content.explanation: When transfer of radar identification is not operationally feasible in an area of radar surveillance, the limitation must be formally addressed. The two authorized methods are: a letter of agreement that states control will not be based on a radar handoff, or direct coordination between the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time. Informal workarounds are not permitted.

operations.problem: The para_context field leads with 'Under 5-4-1' — paragraph-location scaffolding. The activity scenario and choices are otherwise well-constructed and test the controller's decision when a handoff-based control is infeasible for a specified period.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-1: Interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where not operationally feasible. When such constraints exist, they must be covered in an LOA stating control will not be based on a radar handoff, or coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time.

content.situation: Radar surveillance exists, but for a specified period it is not operationally feasible to base control on a radar handoff between your position and the receiving controller.
content.para_context: Under 5-4-1, interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where that is not operationally feasible. When such constraints exist, they must be covered in an LOA stating control will not be based on a radar handoff, or they must be coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time.

choices.text: Use an LOA that clearly states control will not be based on a radar handoff, or coordinate the exception with the other controller for the specified period.
Skip the radar handoff whenever workload is high, without any LOA or coordination, because radar service continues anyway.
Transfer communications first and let the receiving controller determine later whether radar identification was transferred.
Terminate radar service to the aircraft until the constraint no longer exists.

content.explanation: Per 5-4-1, transfers of radar identification are required except where not operationally feasible, and any such exception must be handled by LOA or specific controller coordination.

content.situation: Radar surveillance exists, but for a specified period it is not operationally feasible to base control on a radar handoff between your position and the receiving controller.
content.para_context: Interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where that is not operationally feasible. When such constraints exist, they must be covered in an LOA stating control will not be based on a radar handoff, or they must be coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time.

choices.text: Use an LOA that states control will not be based on a radar handoff, or coordinate the exception with the other controller for the specified period.
Skip the radar handoff whenever workload is high without any LOA or coordination, because radar service continues during communications transfer.
Transfer communications first and let the receiving controller determine later whether radar identification was transferred.
Terminate radar service to the aircraft until the operational constraint no longer exists.

content.explanation: Transfer of radar identification is a general requirement in radar surveillance areas. When it is not operationally feasible, the exception must be formally managed: either through a letter of agreement documenting that control will not be based on a radar handoff, or through direct coordination between the controllers for the specified time period the constraint applies.

operations.problem: The back uses permissive language ('should still be accomplished') where the source uses mandatory language ('must be accomplished'). This weakens a core requirement into a recommendation. The back also buries the primary answer (LOA or coordination) inside a general statement about where transfers are normally required.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-1: Interfacility and intrafacility transfers of radar identification must be accomplished in all areas of radar surveillance except where not operationally feasible. Constraints must be covered in an LOA or coordinated for a specified period.

match.front: What is required when radar identification transfer is not operationally feasible?
match.back: Use an LOA or direct controller coordination for the specified period. In normal radar surveillance areas, interfacility and intrafacility transfers should still be accomplished.

replacements.front: What is required when radar identification transfer is not operationally feasible?
replacements.back: The constraint must be covered in a letter of agreement stating control will not be based on a radar handoff, or coordinated by the transferring and receiving controllers for a specified period of time.

operations.problem: The stem and explanation use paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-4-10', 'Per 5-4-10a') instead of being self-contained operational questions. The item is otherwise correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-10a: only messages specified in this section, a facility directive or LOA are authorized in the fourth line for forwarding control information. Additional control information must be sent by other communication methods.

match.question_text: What control information may be forwarded in the en route fourth line under 5-4-10?

replacements.question_text: What control information may be forwarded in the en route fourth line data block?
replacements.explanation: Only messages specified in the applicable section of the order, a facility directive, or a letter of agreement are authorized in the fourth line for forwarding control information. Any additional control information must be sent via other communications methods.

choices.text: Only messages specified in the section, a facility directive, or an LOA.
Any control information the sector team can abbreviate clearly enough.
Only heading changes; all other data must be verbalized.
Any information that fits in the character field after handoff acceptance.

operations.problem: The stem uses paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-4-10') and the explanation merely restates the correct answer rather than teaching the full operational principle — including that the information must not be modified outside the controller's jurisdiction without verbal coordination or an LOA/facility directive, and the receiving controller must advise if any information is not understood.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-10l: acceptance of a handoff by the receiving controller constitutes receipt of the information contained within the en route fourth line data block. This information must not be modified outside of the controller's area of jurisdiction unless verbally coordinated or specified in a Letter of Agreement or Facility Directive. The receiving controller must advise the transferring controller if any information is not understood or needs to be revised.

match.question_text: What does acceptance of a handoff constitute with respect to the en route fourth line data block under 5-4-10?

replacements.question_text: When a receiving controller accepts a handoff, what does that acceptance mean with respect to the en route fourth line data block?
replacements.explanation: Accepting a handoff constitutes receipt of all information in the en route fourth line data block. This information must not be modified outside the controller's area of jurisdiction unless verbally coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive. If any information is not understood or needs revision, the receiving controller is responsible for advising the transferring controller.

choices.text: Receipt of the information contained in the en route fourth line data block.
Automatic approval for either controller to modify the fourth line outside their area of jurisdiction.
Permission to leave free text in the fourth line after identification transfer begins.
A requirement that all remaining coordination be completed by fourth-line entries only.

operations.problem: The stem begins with paragraph-location scaffolding ('Under 5-4-10,') and the explanation starts with a paragraph reference ('Per 5-4-10a'). The substantive content is correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-10a: Free text may be used by individual sector teams for recording information the team deems appropriate for managing the sector, but must be removed prior to initiation of identification transfer.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-10, free text may be used by individual sector teams for recording information they deem appropriate for managing the sector, but it must be removed prior to initiation of identification transfer.

replacements.question_text: Free text may be used by individual sector teams on the en route fourth line for recording information they deem appropriate for managing the sector, but it must be removed prior to initiation of identification transfer.
replacements.explanation: Free text may be used locally by sector teams for managing the sector, but it must be removed before identification transfer is initiated.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The stem uses paragraph-location scaffolding ('Under 5-4-2') and the explanation cites a paragraph reference. The correct answer is substantially longer than distractors (27 words vs. 8, 12, and 8 words), creating an answer-length cue. The correct answer also embeds both handoff conditions (airspace entry AND communications transfer) redundantly within the answer text.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-2a: A handoff is an action taken to transfer the radar identification of an aircraft from one controller to another controller if the aircraft will enter the receiving controller's airspace and radio communications with the aircraft will be transferred.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-2, what is a handoff?

replacements.question_text: What is a handoff?
replacements.explanation: A handoff transfers radar identification from one controller to another when the aircraft will enter the receiving controller's airspace and radio communications will also be transferred. This distinguishes it from a point out, where radar identification is transferred but communications are not.

choices.text: A transfer of radar identification when the aircraft enters the receiving controller's airspace and communications are transferred.
A transfer of radar identification without any communications transfer.
A coordination used only to issue traffic restrictions, with no airspace entry approval.
A pilot readback used to acknowledge radar service.

operations.problem: The stem uses paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-4-2'). The correct answer omits 'or the appropriate automated system response' from the source definition of Point Out Approved. The source explicitly includes this as an alternative to a communications transfer.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-2d: Point Out Approved — the term used to inform the controller initiating a point out that the aircraft is identified and that approval is granted for the aircraft to enter the receiving controller's airspace, as coordinated, without a communications transfer or the appropriate automated system response.

match.question_text: What does the term 'Point Out Approved' mean under 5-4-2?

replacements.question_text: What does the term 'Point Out Approved' mean?
replacements.explanation: Point Out Approved means the aircraft is identified and approved to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated, without a communications transfer or the appropriate automated system response. It is the receiving controller's acknowledgment that the point out is accepted.

choices.text: The aircraft is identified and approved to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated, without a communications transfer or the appropriate automated system response.
The handoff is complete and the pilot must immediately change frequency.
Traffic separation restrictions are understood and will be complied with.
Radar contact is terminated and responsibility returns to the transferring controller.

operations.problem: The stem begins with paragraph-location scaffolding ('Under 5-4-2,') and the explanation starts with a paragraph reference ('Per 5-4-2c'). The substantive statement is correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-2c: A point out is an action taken by a controller to transfer the radar identification of an aircraft to another controller and radio communications will not be transferred.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-2, a point out transfers radar identification without transferring radio communications.

replacements.question_text: A point out transfers radar identification to another controller without transferring radio communications.
replacements.explanation: A point out transfers radar identification to another controller when the aircraft will enter that controller's airspace, but radio communications are not transferred and remain with the original controller.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The stem uses paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-4-3') and the explanation starts with a paragraph reference ('Per 5-4-3b'). The operational content is correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-3b3: when making a handoff, point out, or issuing traffic restrictions, after the aircraft identification relay the assigned altitude, appropriate restrictions, and information that the aircraft is climbing or descending, if applicable, except when inter/intrafacility directives ensure that the altitude information will be known by the receiving controller.

match.question_text: When making a handoff, point out, or issuing traffic restrictions under 5-4-3, what information is normally relayed immediately after the aircraft identification?

replacements.question_text: When making a handoff, point out, or issuing traffic restrictions, what information is normally relayed immediately after the aircraft identification?
replacements.explanation: After the aircraft identification, relay the assigned altitude, applicable restrictions, and whether the aircraft is climbing or descending if applicable. This step may be omitted when inter- or intrafacility directives ensure the receiving controller already knows the altitude information.

choices.text: Assigned altitude, applicable restrictions, and climb or descent information if applicable.
The next frequency and communications transfer point.
The receiving controller's preferred coordination method.
Only the route of flight, because altitude is inferred from the data block in all cases.

operations.problem: The explanation starts with a paragraph reference ('Per 5-4-3') rather than teaching the operational principle in a self-contained way. The stem and choices are correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-3a: Transfer the radar identification of an aircraft by at least one of the following methods: physically point to the target on the receiving controller's display, use landline voice communications, or use automation capabilities.

match.question_text: Which is an approved method for transferring radar identification?

replacements.question_text: Which is an approved method for transferring radar identification?
replacements.explanation: Radar identification may be transferred by physically pointing to the target on the receiving controller's display, using landline voice communications, or using approved automation capabilities. Automated handoff capabilities are only available when flight data processing is operational.

choices.text: Use landline voice communications, physical pointing, or approved automation capabilities.
Rely on pilot position reports alone without controller-to-controller transfer.
Use tower Quick Look functions whenever convenient, even without a facility directive.
Transfer identity only after the aircraft changes to the new frequency.

operations.problem: The stem uses paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 5-4-3') and the explanation starts with a paragraph reference. The operational content is correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-3a: physically point to the target on the receiving controller's display is one of the approved methods for transferring radar identification.

match.question_text: Which of the following is an authorized method to transfer radar identification under 5-4-3?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is an authorized method to transfer radar identification?
replacements.explanation: Physically pointing to the target on the receiving controller's display is an approved method for transferring radar identification. Other approved methods include landline voice communications and automation capabilities.

choices.text: Physically point to the target on the receiving controller's display.
Instruct the pilot to ident and let the receiving controller assume the correct target.
Transfer communications and wait for the receiving controller to call back if the target is uncertain.
Use Quick Look between any terminal positions without a facility directive.

operations.problem: The explanation starts with a paragraph reference ('Per the note in 5-4-3b4') rather than teaching the operational exception in a self-contained way.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-3 note after b4: When physically pointing to the target, you do not have to state the aircraft position.

match.question_text: When physically pointing to the target during a handoff or point out, you still must state the aircraft position.

replacements.question_text: When physically pointing to the target during a handoff or point out, you still must state the aircraft position.
replacements.explanation: When physically pointing to the target on the receiving controller's display, you do not have to state the aircraft position. Physical pointing substitutes for the verbal position report in the coordination sequence.

content.instruction: One element in this fourth-line coordination entry is incorrect. Tap it.
content.explanation: Per 5-4-10d, assigned headings in the en route fourth line must use the designation character 'H' preceding a three-digit number.

content.instruction: One element in this fourth-line coordination entry is incorrect. Tap it.
content.explanation: Assigned headings in the en route fourth line data block must use the designation character 'H' preceding a three-digit number (e.g., H080). The character 'S' is used for assigned airspeeds, not headings.

operations.problem: The para_context field embeds a paragraph-location reference ('Under 5-4-2') and the explanation starts with a paragraph reference ('Per 5-4-2'). The situational content and choices are well-constructed.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-2c and 5-4-2d: A point out transfers radar identification without transferring radio communications. Point Out Approved means the aircraft is identified and approved to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated, without a communications transfer.

content.situation: You need another controller to allow an identified aircraft into that controller's airspace, but radio communications will remain with you. A trainee starts a handoff because the target is already identified on both displays.
content.para_context: Under 5-4-2, a point out is used to transfer radar identification to another controller when radio communications will not be transferred. 'Point Out Approved' means the aircraft is identified and approved to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated, without a communications transfer.

choices.text: Use a point out and obtain point out approval, because communications will remain with your position.
Initiate a handoff, because every transfer of radar identification requires a communications transfer.
Issue traffic only, because point outs are used only after communications have already transferred.
Do nothing, because the other controller may not approve entry unless the pilot is already on the receiving frequency.

content.explanation: Per 5-4-2, a point out is the action used when radar identification is transferred but radio communications are not.

content.situation: You need another controller to allow an identified aircraft into that controller's airspace, but radio communications will remain with you. A trainee starts a handoff because the target is already identified on both displays.
content.para_context: A point out transfers radar identification to another controller when radio communications will not be transferred. Point Out Approved means the aircraft is identified and approved to enter the receiving controller's airspace as coordinated, without a communications transfer.

choices.text: Use a point out and obtain point out approval, because communications will remain with your position.
Initiate a handoff, because every transfer of radar identification requires a communications transfer.
Issue traffic only, because point outs are used only after communications have already transferred.
Do nothing, because the other controller may not approve entry unless the pilot is already on the receiving frequency.

content.explanation: A point out is the correct action when radar identification must be transferred but radio communications remain with the original controller. The receiving controller responds with 'Point Out Approved' to acknowledge identification and grant airspace entry.

operations.source_basis: 5-4-3b2(c): EN ROUTE. The Computer Identification Number (CID) during intrafacility point outs may be used as the aircraft identification. Acceptance of a point out using the discrete beacon code as the aircraft's identification constitutes agreement per 5-4-3 note after b2(b).

choices.text: Use landline voice communications, physical pointing, or approved automation capabilities.

content.question_text: During en route intrafacility point outs, what may be used as the aircraft identification in addition to the call sign?

choices.text: The Computer Identification Number (CID).
The discrete beacon code, which may be used for any en route point out.
The aircraft type designator only.
The assigned squawk code, regardless of facility agreement.

content.explanation: For en route intrafacility point outs, the Computer Identification Number (CID) may be used as the aircraft identification. The discrete beacon code is authorized only for interfacility point outs and only when both controllers agree; acceptance of a point out using the beacon code constitutes that agreement.

operations.problem: The explanation merely repeats the assembled target phrase instead of teaching the phraseology format so the student can construct similar transmissions independently. The activity content (word bank, sequence, target phrase) is otherwise correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-3b phraseology: HANDOFF/POINT-OUT/TRAFFIC (aircraft position), (aircraft ID or discrete beacon code), (altitude, restrictions, and other pertinent information, if applicable).

content.situation: You are making a point out on an aircraft southwest of Richmond VOR. The aircraft is Southwest 452 at 5,000 feet. Build the point-out transmission.
content.target_phrase: POINT OUT SOUTHWEST OF RICHMOND V O R SOUTHWEST FOUR FIFTY-TWO FIVE THOUSAND
content.explanation: The correct transmission is "POINT OUT SOUTHWEST OF RICHMOND V O R SOUTHWEST FOUR FIFTY-TWO FIVE THOUSAND."

content.situation: You are making a point out on an aircraft southwest of Richmond VOR. The aircraft is Southwest 452 at 5,000 feet. Build the point-out transmission.
content.target_phrase: POINT OUT SOUTHWEST OF RICHMOND V O R SOUTHWEST FOUR FIFTY-TWO FIVE THOUSAND
content.explanation: The point out transmission follows the format: POINT OUT (aircraft position), (aircraft ID), (altitude). State the position relative to a known fix (southwest of Richmond VOR), then the aircraft call sign (Southwest 452), then the altitude (5,000).

operations.problem: The explanation starts with a paragraph reference ('Per 5-4-3b') instead of teaching the coordination sequence in a self-contained way. The steps and their order are correct.

content.instruction: Place these coordination items in the correct order.

steps.text: State the target position relative to a fix, map symbol, or known radar target.
State the assigned altitude, applicable restrictions, and climb or descent information if applicable.
Provide any other pertinent information not already contained in the data block or available flight data.

content.explanation: Per 5-4-3b, controller-to-controller coordination is relayed in the order of position, identification, altitude or restrictions if applicable, then other pertinent information.

content.instruction: Place these coordination items in the correct order.

steps.text: State the target position relative to a fix, map symbol, or known radar target.
State the assigned altitude, applicable restrictions, and climb or descent information if applicable.

operations.problem: The explanation uses paragraph-location scaffolding ('Per 5-4-3c') instead of teaching the response terminology in a self-contained way. The activity content (display text, tokens, error) is correct.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-3c: when receiving a point out, respond with POINT OUT APPROVED. TRAFFIC OBSERVED is the correct response when receiving traffic restrictions, not a point out.

content.display_text: POINT OUT OBSERVED
content.explanation: Per 5-4-3c, the correct acknowledgment for a point out is 'POINT OUT APPROVED.' 'TRAFFIC OBSERVED' is a different response.

content.explanation: The correct acknowledgment for a point out is 'POINT OUT APPROVED.' 'TRAFFIC OBSERVED' is the response used when receiving traffic restrictions, not a point out. The two response terms correspond to different coordination actions.

The explanation also unnecessarily cites a paragraph identifier.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-5b requires the transferring controller to verbally obtain the receiving controller's approval prior to making any changes to flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information while the handoff is being initiated or after acceptance, unless otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive.

match.question_text: Once a handoff has been initiated or accepted, when may the transferring controller change the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block without further approval?

replacements.question_text: Once a handoff has been initiated or accepted, when may the transferring controller change the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block without further approval?
replacements.explanation: Unless otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive, the transferring controller must verbally obtain the receiving controller's approval prior to making any changes to an aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information while the handoff is being initiated or after acceptance.

choices.text: Only when otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive.
Any time before the pilot checks in with the receiving controller.
Any time the aircraft remains above the minimum vectoring altitude.
Only after the aircraft physically enters the receiving sector.

operations.problem: The stem includes a paragraph-location reference ('Under 5-4-5') and the explanation cites the paragraph identifier ('Per 5-4-5a') instead of teaching the handoff timing requirement operationally.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-5a requires the transferring controller to complete a handoff prior to an aircraft entering the airspace delegated to the receiving controller, unless otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-5, when must the transferring controller complete a handoff unless otherwise coordinated or specified?

replacements.question_text: Unless otherwise coordinated or specified, when must the transferring controller complete a handoff?
replacements.explanation: The transferring controller must complete the handoff before the aircraft enters the airspace delegated to the receiving controller, unless otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: Before the aircraft enters the airspace delegated to the receiving controller.
Only after the aircraft checks in on the receiving controller's frequency.
Any time before the aircraft exits the receiving controller's sector.
After the aircraft is established in the receiving controller's airspace and separation is assured.

operations.problem: This question tests the same operational rule (5-4-5b) as item 8f489997 with substantially overlapping stems. Both ask about post-handoff changes to flight path, altitude, speed, or data block. One item suffices to test this concept, and 8f489997 is the better item because it tests the exception condition which demands higher-order operational judgment.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-5b is already tested by item 8f489997; the two items assess the same operational rule with substantially overlapping stems and answer domains.

match.question_text: What must the transferring controller do before changing an aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information while a handoff is being initiated or after acceptance under 5-4-5?

operations.problem: The stem prepends 'Under 5-4-5' to a verbatim source quote, and the explanation cites the paragraph identifier ('Per 5-4-5f') instead of teaching the operational duty.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-5f states the transferring controller must advise the receiving controller if radar monitoring is required.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-5, the transferring controller must advise the receiving controller if radar monitoring is required.

replacements.question_text: The transferring controller must advise the receiving controller if radar monitoring is required.
