choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: This activity uses an identical stem to question 8f489997, testing the same source statement (5-4-5b) without varying the operational angle or tested skill. The correct answer also exhibits a length cue relative to distractors. The replacement tests an uncovered aspect of the transferring controller's duties (subparagraph c) to provide genuine content coverage rather than copy-paste duplication.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-5c lists six categories of pertinent information the transferring controller must advise when not contained in the data block or flight progress strip: assigned heading, airspeed restrictions, altitude information issued, observed track or deviation, beacon code if different, and any other pertinent information.

content.question_text: Once a handoff has been initiated or accepted, when may the transferring controller change the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block without further approval?

choices.text: Only when otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive; otherwise the receiving controller's approval must be obtained first.
Any time before the pilot checks on with the receiving controller.
Any time the aircraft remains above the MVA.
Only after the aircraft enters the receiving sector physically.

content.explanation: The transferring controller must get the receiving controller's approval before making post-handoff changes unless a standing procedure allows it.

content.question_text: When the transferring controller advises the receiving controller of information not contained in the data block or flight progress strip, which of the following must be included?

choices.text: Assigned heading.
The aircraft's filed cruising speed.
The last recorded barometric pressure setting.
The beacon code, regardless of whether it changed since the last coordination.

content.explanation: The transferring controller must advise the receiving controller of assigned heading, airspeed restrictions, altitude information issued, observed track or deviation from the last route clearance, the beacon code if different from that normally used or previously coordinated, and any other pertinent information not contained in the data block or flight progress strip.

operations.problem: The back compresses four distinct transferring-controller duties (complete handoff before airspace entry, coordinate post-handoff changes, pass pertinent info and restrictions, resolve conflicts before communications transfer) into a single sentence. This overloads the card with multiple retrieval targets drawn from different subparagraphs, diluting recall of any one duty.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-5h requires the transferring controller, prior to transferring communications, to resolve potential violations and conflicts, coordinate with controllers whose airspace the aircraft will transit, forward issued restrictions, and comply with receiving controller restrictions.

match.front: What must the transferring controller do during a handoff?
match.back: Complete the handoff before delegated-airspace entry, coordinate any post-handoff changes, pass pertinent info and restrictions, and resolve conflicts before communications transfer.

replacements.front: Before transferring communications, what must the transferring controller do?
replacements.back: Resolve potential violations of adjacent airspace and conflicts with other aircraft in your area of jurisdiction, coordinate with any controller whose airspace the aircraft will transit, forward issued restrictions to the receiving controller, and comply with restrictions issued by the receiving controller.

operations.problem: The stem includes a paragraph-location reference ('under 5-4-6') and the explanation cites the paragraph identifier ('Per 5-4-6a') instead of teaching the receiving controller's target verification duty operationally.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-6a requires the receiving controller to ensure target position correspondence or appropriate automated data block association before accepting a handoff.

match.question_text: Before accepting a handoff under 5-4-6, what must the receiving controller ensure?

replacements.question_text: Before accepting a handoff, what must the receiving controller ensure?
replacements.explanation: The receiving controller must ensure that the target position corresponds with the position given by the transferring controller or that there is an appropriate association between an automated data block and the target being transferred before accepting the handoff.

choices.text: That the target position corresponds with the transferring controller's information or that the automated association is appropriate.
That the pilot has already been transferred to your frequency.
That the data block shows a full route of flight and requested altitude.
That the aircraft has crossed the sector boundary before you accept the handoff.

operations.problem: The stem includes a paragraph-location reference ('under 5-4-6'), and the explanation is thin and cites the paragraph identifier ('Per 5-4-6f3') instead of explaining the operational significance of these data block indicators and why verbal coordination is required.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-6f3 requires the receiving controller to initiate verbal coordination when DATA, CST, NONE, or OLD is displayed in the data block before accepting control of a track.

match.question_text: What action is required under 5-4-6 when 'DATA,' 'CST,' 'NONE,' or 'OLD' is displayed in the data block before accepting control of a track?

replacements.question_text: When 'DATA,' 'CST,' 'NONE,' or 'OLD' is displayed in the data block before accepting control of a track, what action is required?
replacements.explanation: These data block indicators signal that the automated handoff information may be incomplete or unreliable. The receiving controller must initiate verbal coordination before accepting control of the track when DATA, CST, NONE, or OLD is displayed.

choices.text: Initiate verbal coordination.
Accept control and wait to see whether the indication clears automatically.
Delete the data block and rely on primary radar only.
Tell the pilot to recycle the transponder before accepting the handoff.

operations.problem: The stem prepends 'Under 5-4-6' to a long, verbatim restatement of the confirmation note, and the explanation cites a paragraph identifier ('Per the note under 5-4-6e2') instead of teaching the confirmation rule in operational terms.
operations.source_basis: The note under 5-4-6e2 states observing code removal from the data block when an aircraft changes to the computer assigned code satisfies beacon target confirmation requirements.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-6, if a displayed discrete code is automatically removed from the data block when the aircraft changes to the computer assigned code, that observation satisfies the beacon target confirmation requirement.

replacements.question_text: An observation of a discrete code being automatically removed from the data block when an aircraft changes to the computer assigned code satisfies the beacon target confirmation requirement.
replacements.explanation: When an aircraft changes to the computer assigned beacon code and the previously displayed non-matching code is automatically removed from the data block, observing that removal satisfies the beacon target identity confirmation requirement.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The correct answer is notably longer than the three distractors, creating an answer-length cue that can reveal the correct choice to a test-wise student. The correct answer must express a two-part verification duty from the source (position match OR automated association), so the remedy balances by slightly lengthening distractors while keeping the correct answer concise.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-6a requires the receiving controller to verify that the target position corresponds with the transferring controller's information or that automated data block association is appropriate before accepting a handoff.

content.question_text: Before accepting a handoff, what must the receiving controller verify?

choices.text: That the target position matches the transferring controller's description or that the automated association is appropriate.
That the pilot has already acknowledged the next frequency.
That the aircraft has crossed the sector boundary.
That wake turbulence separation has been delegated to tower.

content.explanation: The receiving controller must ensure the target association is correct before accepting the handoff.

content.question_text: Before accepting a handoff, what must the receiving controller verify?

choices.text: That the target position matches the transferring controller's description, or an appropriate automated data block association exists.
That the pilot has already acknowledged the assigned next frequency on the new sector.
That the aircraft has already crossed the sector boundary on the radar display.
That wake turbulence separation responsibility has been delegated to the control tower.

content.explanation: The receiving controller must verify that the target position corresponds with the transferring controller's information or that there is an appropriate association between the automated data block and the target being transferred before accepting the handoff.

operations.problem: The para_context field contains a paragraph-location reference ('Under 5-4-6g'), and the explanation embeds 'Per 5-4-6g.' Both should convey the operational requirement without teaching paragraph-number trivia.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-6g requires the receiving controller to notify the OS/CIC when a MISM is displayed in the data block while working ERAM.

content.situation: You are working ERAM and an accepted handoff displays 'MISM' in the data block.
content.para_context: Under 5-4-6g, the receiving controller must notify the OS/CIC when a MISM is displayed in the data block.

choices.text: Notify the OS/CIC that a MISM is displayed in the data block.
Ignore it unless the pilot reports a navigation problem.
Immediately terminate radar service to the aircraft.
Tell the pilot to recycle the transponder before coordinating with anyone else.

content.situation: You are working ERAM and an accepted handoff displays 'MISM' in the data block.
content.para_context: The receiving controller must notify the OS/CIC when a MISM is displayed in the data block while working ERAM.

choices.text: Notify the OS/CIC that a MISM is displayed in the data block.
Ignore it unless the pilot reports a navigation problem.
Immediately terminate radar service to the aircraft.
Tell the pilot to recycle the transponder before coordinating with anyone else.

content.explanation: In ERAM, a MISM indication in the data block means the system has detected a mismatch. The receiving controller must notify the OS/CIC when this indication is displayed.

operations.problem: Stem embeds 'under 5-4-7' which teaches paragraph-location trivia. Explanation merely restates the answer with a paragraph citation without teaching the operational requirement or its conditions.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-7a1: The transferring controller must obtain approval before permitting an aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace. EN ROUTE automated approval may be used in lieu of verbal; if the receiving controller takes no action, revert to verbal.

match.question_text: Before permitting an aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace under 5-4-7, what must the transferring controller obtain?

replacements.question_text: Before permitting an aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace on a point out, what must the transferring controller obtain?
replacements.explanation: The transferring controller must obtain the receiving controller's approval before the aircraft enters the delegated airspace. For en route operations, automated approval may be used in place of verbal approval, but if the receiving controller takes no action, the transferring controller must revert to verbal coordination procedures.

choices.text: Approval from the receiving controller.
A pilot readback confirming the next frequency.
A new beacon code assignment for the aircraft.
A supervisor's approval to begin communications transfer.

operations.problem: Stem embeds 'under 5-4-7' as paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation cites a sub-paragraph reference without teaching the operational contingency that triggers the fallback.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-7a1(a): For EN ROUTE automated point outs, automated approval may be used in lieu of verbal approval. If the receiving controller takes no action, revert to verbal procedures.

match.question_text: If an en route automated point out receives no action from the receiving controller under 5-4-7, what must the transferring controller do?

replacements.question_text: If an en route automated point out receives no action from the receiving controller, what must the transferring controller do?
replacements.explanation: Automated point out approval in the en route environment is not automatic. If the receiving controller takes no action on the automated point out, the transferring controller must revert to verbal coordination procedures. Silence does not constitute approval.

choices.text: Revert to verbal procedures.
Treat the lack of response as automatic approval.
Transfer communications and wait for the receiving controller to respond after check-in.
Convert the point out into prearranged coordination without further action.

operations.problem: Stem leads with 'Under 5-4-7' as paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation only cites a paragraph reference rather than teaching that approval after the initial point out remains a separate obligation.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-7a2: The transferring controller must obtain the receiving controller's approval before making any changes to an aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information after the point out has been approved.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-7, once a point out has been approved, the transferring controller must obtain the receiving controller's approval before changing the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information.

replacements.question_text: Once a point out has been approved, the transferring controller must obtain the receiving controller's approval before changing the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information.
replacements.explanation: After a point out has been approved, any subsequent change to the aircraft's flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information requires the receiving controller's separate approval. The initial point out approval does not authorize later changes without further coordination.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: Stem embeds 'Under 5-4-8' as paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation cites paragraph references instead of teaching that all three conditions must be satisfied concurrently.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-8: Transfer radar identification, altitude control, and/or en route fourth line control information without verbal coordination under these conditions: a. During radar handoff; and b. Via information displayed in full data blocks; and c. When following procedures specified in your facility AIT directive and/or LOA.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-8, when may automated information transfer be used without verbal coordination?

replacements.question_text: When may automated information transfer be used without verbal coordination?
replacements.explanation: Automated information transfer without verbal coordination requires all three conditions to be met: the transfer must occur during radar handoff, the information must be displayed in full data blocks, and the transfer must follow procedures specified in the facility AIT directive or LOA. If any condition is missing, verbal coordination is required.

choices.text: During radar handoff, via information displayed in full data blocks, and while following procedures in a facility AIT directive or LOA.
Any time both controllers can see the target, even if no AIT directive or LOA exists.
Only after the pilot has checked in on the receiving controller's frequency.
Only when the transferring controller also sends verbal coordination as backup.

operations.problem: Stem asks what is 'specifically stated in 5-4-8' — paragraph-location premise. Explanation references paragraph number without teaching why full data blocks matter for the AIT function.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-8b: Automated information transfer without verbal coordination requires information displayed in full data blocks.

match.question_text: Which display requirement is specifically stated in 5-4-8 for using automated information transfer without verbal coordination?

replacements.question_text: What display configuration is required for automated information transfer without verbal coordination?
replacements.explanation: For automated information transfer to be used without verbal coordination, the transferred information must be displayed in full data blocks. Limited data blocks, free-text entries on the fourth line, or single-controller scope display do not meet this display requirement.

choices.text: The information must be displayed in full data blocks.
The information may be displayed in limited data blocks so long as the target is correlated.
The information must be written in free text on the fourth line.
The information must be displayed only on the transferring controller's scope.

operations.problem: Stem leads with 'Under 5-4-8' as paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation only cites paragraph reference without teaching that the AIT directive or LOA is a mandatory precondition.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-8c: Automated information transfer without verbal coordination must follow procedures specified in the facility AIT directive and/or LOA.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-8, automated information transfer may be used without verbal coordination even if no facility AIT directive or LOA covers the procedure.

replacements.question_text: Automated information transfer may be used without verbal coordination even if no facility AIT directive or LOA covers the procedure.
replacements.explanation: A facility AIT directive or LOA that covers the specific procedure is a mandatory condition for using automated information transfer without verbal coordination. Even when a radar handoff is in progress and full data blocks are displayed, verbal coordination procedures must be used if no applicable directive or LOA authorizes the transfer.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: Stem embeds 'under 5-4-9' as paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation mentions JO 7210.3 but does not clarify that without a published facility directive, prearranged coordination is not authorized.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-9: Prearranged coordination allowing aircraft under your control to enter another controller's area of jurisdiction may only be approved provided procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with FAA Order JO 7210.3.

match.question_text: When may prearranged coordination be approved under 5-4-9?

replacements.question_text: When may prearranged coordination be approved for aircraft entering another controller's area of jurisdiction?
replacements.explanation: Prearranged coordination may only be approved when the procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with FAA Order JO 7210.3. Without a published facility directive, other proper coordination methods such as radar handoff, AIT, verbal coordination, or point out must be used.

choices.text: Only when procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with JO 7210.3.
Whenever both controllers have informally agreed to use it on that shift.
Any time traffic volume is high enough to justify faster coordination.
Whenever radar handoff is unavailable, regardless of local directives.

operations.problem: Stem asks 'Which of the following is listed in the 5-4-9 note' — the entire question premise is paragraph-location trivia, not operational knowledge. The student is tested on what appears in a specific note rather than on recognizing proper coordination methods.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-9 Note: Coordination can be accomplished by several means: radar handoff, automated information transfer, verbal, point-out, and by prearranged coordination procedures identified in a facility directive. Under no circumstances may one controller permit an aircraft to enter another's airspace without proper coordination.

match.question_text: Which of the following is listed in the 5-4-9 note as a proper means of coordination?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is a proper means of coordination for transferring an aircraft into another controller's airspace?
replacements.explanation: Proper means of coordination include radar handoff, automated information transfer, verbal coordination, point out, and prearranged coordination procedures published in a facility directive. Allowing an aircraft to enter another controller's airspace and coordinating only if a conflict later develops, waiting for a pilot call, or treating controller silence as approval are not proper coordination methods.

choices.text: Point out.
Waiting for the pilot to call the next sector and advise position.
Allowing entry first and coordinating only if traffic becomes a factor.
Treating silence from the other controller as automatic approval.

operations.problem: Stem leads with 'Under 5-4-9' as paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation only cites the note by reference rather than teaching the absolute nature of the coordination requirement.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-9 Note: Under no circumstances may one controller permit an aircraft to enter another's airspace without proper coordination. Coordination can be accomplished by several means: radar handoff, AIT, verbal, point-out, and prearranged coordination.

match.question_text: Under 5-4-9, one controller may never permit an aircraft to enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination.

replacements.question_text: One controller may never permit an aircraft to enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination.
replacements.explanation: Under no circumstances may a controller permit an aircraft to enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination. Proper coordination methods include radar handoff, automated information transfer, verbal coordination, point out, and prearranged coordination procedures published in a facility directive.

False

content.question_text: The receiving controller is responsible for separation between point out aircraft and other aircraft for which the receiving controller has separation responsibility.

operations.problem: Para_context field and explanation both embed paragraph-location scaffolding ('Under 5-4-7a2', 'Per 5-4-7a2'). The explanation should teach the operational rule without citing a sub-paragraph reference.

operations.source_basis: 5-4-9: Prearranged coordination may only be approved when procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with JO 7210.3. 5-4-9 Note: Under no circumstances may one controller permit an aircraft to enter another's airspace without proper coordination.

content.situation: You want to let an aircraft under your control enter another controller's area of jurisdiction using prearranged coordination, but no published facility directive establishes the required procedures.
content.para_context: Under 5-4-9, prearranged coordination allowing aircraft under your control to enter another controller's area of jurisdiction may only be approved when procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with FAA Order JO 7210.3. Under no circumstances may one controller permit an aircraft to enter another's airspace without proper coordination.

choices.text: Do not use prearranged coordination in that situation; use a proper coordination method unless published facility procedures authorize the prearranged process.
Use prearranged coordination anyway because efficient traffic movement takes priority over publication requirements.
Let the aircraft enter the other controller's airspace and coordinate only if a conflict later develops.
Transfer communications first and treat the check-in as implied coordination.

content.explanation: Per 5-4-9, prearranged coordination is allowed only when procedures are established and published in a facility directive; aircraft may not enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination.

content.situation: You want to let an aircraft under your control enter another controller's area of jurisdiction using prearranged coordination, but no published facility directive establishes the required procedures.
content.para_context: Prearranged coordination allowing aircraft under your control to enter another controller's area of jurisdiction may only be approved when procedures are established and published in a facility directive in accordance with FAA Order JO 7210.3. Under no circumstances may one controller permit an aircraft to enter another's airspace without proper coordination.

choices.text: Do not use prearranged coordination in that situation; use a proper coordination method unless published facility procedures authorize the prearranged process.
Use prearranged coordination anyway because efficient traffic movement takes priority over publication requirements.
Let the aircraft enter the other controller's airspace and coordinate only if a conflict later develops.
Transfer communications first and treat the check-in as implied coordination.

content.explanation: Prearranged coordination requires published procedures in a facility directive. Without them, the controller must use another proper coordination method such as radar handoff, automated information transfer, verbal coordination, or point out. An aircraft may never enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination.

operations.problem: The flashcard back includes 'provide the required identification and altitude information' and 'advise when the point out is no longer needed' — neither requirement appears in the 5-4-7 source blocks. The assigned source blocks cover only approval before entry, approval after approval for changes, compliance with restrictions, and handoff/transfer responsibilities.
operations.source_basis: 5-4-7a1: The transferring controller must obtain approval before permitting an aircraft to enter the receiving controller's delegated airspace. The source blocks for this paragraph do not include identification/altitude information requirements or point-out termination advisories; those belong to other paragraphs.

match.front: What is required for a point out into another controller's delegated airspace?
match.back: Obtain approval before the aircraft enters that airspace, provide the required identification and altitude information, and advise when the point out is no longer needed.

replacements.front: What is required for a point out into another controller's delegated airspace?
replacements.back: The transferring controller must obtain the receiving controller's approval before the aircraft enters that delegated airspace.

operations.problem: Stem cites 'under 5-5-1' — paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation is a restatement of the correct answer prefixed with 'Per 5-5-1a.' operations.source_basis: 5-5-1a: 'Radar separation must be applied to all RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.' Exception: 'point-to-point routes via an impromptu route.'

replacements.question_text: To which RNAV aircraft must radar separation be applied?
replacements.explanation: Radar separation must be applied to all RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes. Oceanic airspace is excluded from this requirement. Point-to-point routes via an impromptu route are excepted.

choices.text: All RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.
Only RNAV aircraft operating above FL450 on Q routes.
Only RNAV aircraft operating on published ATS routes inside oceanic airspace.
Only point-to-point RNAV routes via an impromptu route.

operations.problem: Stem cites 'under 5-5-1' — paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation is a restatement of the answer prefixed with 'Per 5-5-1b2' with no teaching of the operational condition.
operations.source_basis: 5-5-1b2: 'An aircraft taking off and another radar identified aircraft when the aircraft taking off will be radar-identified within 1 mile of the runway end.'

match.question_text: When may radar separation be applied between an aircraft taking off and another radar-identified aircraft under 5-5-1?

replacements.question_text: When may radar separation be applied between an aircraft taking off and another radar-identified aircraft?
replacements.explanation: Radar separation may be applied between an aircraft taking off and another radar-identified aircraft when the aircraft taking off will be radar-identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

choices.text: When the aircraft taking off will be radar-identified within 1 mile of the runway end.
Only after the aircraft taking off is 3 miles from the runway end.
Only after the departure has reported established on course.
Only when both aircraft are already radar identified and level at the same altitude.

operations.problem: This true/false question tests the same 5-5-1b2 departure rule already tested by question c0a4d56b, and embeds paragraph-location scaffolding in both stem and explanation. The operational content of 5-5-1b3 (five mandatory conditions for radar separation between radar-identified and non-radar-identified aircraft during climb/descend) is entirely uncovered by any item in this shard.
operations.source_basis: 5-5-1b3: 'A radar-identified aircraft and one not radar-identified when either is cleared to climb/descend through the altitude of the other provided: (a) The performance of the radar system is adequate... (b) Flight data on the aircraft not radar-identified indicate it is a type which can be expected to give adequate primary... return... (c) The airspace within which radar separation is applied is not less than the following number of miles from the edge of the radar display: (1) When less than 40 miles from the antenna — 6 miles... (d) Radar separation is maintained between the radar-identified aircraft and all observed primary... and secondary radar targets until nonradar separation is established... (e) When the aircraft involved are on the same relative heading, the radar-identified aircraft is vectored a sufficient distance from the route of the aircraft not radar identified to assure the targets are not superimposed prior to issuing the clearance to climb/descend.'

match.question_text: Under 5-5-1, radar separation may be applied between an aircraft taking off and another radar-identified aircraft if the departure will be radar identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

replacements.question_text: When radar separation is applied between a radar-identified aircraft and one not radar-identified during a climb or descent, what is the minimum required distance from the edge of the radar display when the aircraft are less than 40 miles from the antenna?
replacements.explanation: When applying radar separation between a radar-identified aircraft and one not radar-identified during a climb or descent, the airspace must not be less than 6 miles from the edge of the radar display when less than 40 miles from the antenna.

choices.text: 6 miles.
3 miles.
10 miles.
1 1/2 miles.

operations.problem: This table_lookup activity asks students to identify which referenced table (TBL 2-3-10) supports aircraft equipment suffix lookup. The task tests document-navigation trivia — recognizing a cross-reference citation — rather than any operational ATC skill. Students are not asked to use the table to determine an equipment suffix or apply it to a separation decision; they are merely asked to name which table is cited. This teaches paragraph-location habits, not ATC substance.

TBL 14-2-1, CPDLC Response Attribute.

5 miles only if the aircraft is VFR.

operations.problem: Stem opens with 'Under 5-5-10' — paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation is prefixed with 'Per 5-5-10c' and mixes the rule statement with the paragraph citation. The true/false format and substance are otherwise appropriate for testing this specific operational restriction.

operations.problem: Para_context field opens with 'Under 5-5-10d' — paragraph-location scaffolding. Explanation is prefixed with 'Per 5-5-10d.' The situation, choices, and operational reasoning are otherwise sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-5-10d: 'VFR aircraft approaching Class B, Class C, Class D, or TRSA airspace which is under the control jurisdiction of another air traffic control facility should either be provided with a radar handoff or be advised that radar service is terminated, given their position in relation to the ... airspace, and the ATC frequency, if known, for the airspace to be entered. These actions should be accomplished in sufficient time for the pilot to obtain the required ATC approval prior to entering the airspace involved, or to avoid the airspace.'

content.situation: A VFR aircraft under your radar service is approaching Class D airspace controlled by another facility. You have not coordinated a radar handoff yet, and the aircraft will need ATC approval before entering that airspace.
content.para_context: Under 5-5-10d, a VFR aircraft approaching Class B, C, D, or TRSA airspace under another facility's control should either receive a radar handoff or be advised that radar service is terminated, given its position relative to the airspace, and given the ATC frequency if known, in sufficient time to obtain approval or avoid the airspace.

choices.text: Provide a radar handoff, or terminate radar service and advise the pilot of position and the frequency if known, in time to obtain approval or avoid the airspace.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the boundary, then let the pilot call the next facility without any advance advisory.
Allow the aircraft to enter and coordinate only if the other facility objects.
Keep the aircraft on your frequency and advise that Class D entry approval is not required for VFR targets in radar service.

content.explanation: Per 5-5-10d, this action must be taken early enough for the pilot to obtain the required ATC approval prior to entry or avoid the airspace.

content.situation: A VFR aircraft under your radar service is approaching Class D airspace controlled by another facility. You have not coordinated a radar handoff yet, and the aircraft will need ATC approval before entering that airspace.

operations.source_basis: 5-5-11: 'Separate a radar-controlled aircraft climbing or descending through the altitude of an aircraft that has been tracked to the edge of the scope/display by the following minima until nonradar separation has been established.'

replacements.question_text: The required edge-of-scope minimum is maintained until nonradar separation has been established.
replacements.explanation: Edge-of-scope separation minima apply until nonradar separation has been established between the aircraft involved. The minimum is not indefinite — it is a bridge requirement pending establishment of nonradar separation.

False

content.situation: A radar-controlled aircraft is climbing through the altitude of another aircraft that has already been tracked to the edge of the scope. The climb occurs when the aircraft are 42 miles from the antenna, and nonradar separation has not yet been established.
content.para_context: Under 5-5-11, separate a radar-controlled aircraft climbing or descending through the altitude of an aircraft tracked to the edge of scope by 3 miles from the edge when less than 40 miles from the antenna, and 5 miles from the edge when 40 miles or more from the antenna, until nonradar separation is established.

choices.text: Maintain 5 miles from the edge of scope until nonradar separation has been established.
Maintain 3 miles from the edge because edge-of-scope separation never changes with antenna distance.
Use 1.5 miles from the edge because adjacent-airspace minima apply.
Discontinue separation once the target reaches the edge of scope because it is no longer displayed.

content.explanation: Per 5-5-11b, when the aircraft are 40 miles or more from the antenna, use 5 miles from the edge of scope until nonradar separation is established.

content.situation: A radar-controlled aircraft is climbing through the altitude of another aircraft that has already been tracked to the edge of the scope. The climb occurs when the aircraft are 42 miles from the antenna, and nonradar separation has not yet been established.

choices.text: Maintain 5 miles from the edge of scope until nonradar separation has been established.
Maintain 3 miles from the edge because edge-of-scope separation never changes with antenna distance.
Use 1.5 miles from the edge because adjacent-airspace minima apply.
Discontinue separation once the target reaches the edge of scope because it is no longer displayed.

content.explanation: When the aircraft are 40 miles or more from the antenna, use 5 miles from the edge of scope until nonradar separation is established.

The same operational knowledge is tested more effectively by the multiple-choice question 17fa2e86, which requires discriminating the correct rule from plausible alternatives such as ends-vs-centers confusion and the incorrect claim that digital targets are advisory. The T/F adds no distinct retrieval value and the explanation is merely a tautology.
operations.source_basis: JO 7110.65 5-5-2d requires applying radar separation on all-digital displays between the centers of digital targets, and digital targets must not be allowed to touch.

operations.problem: The flashcard back omits two elements from the source: sub-rule c (apply separation between the end of a beacon control slash and the center of a primary target) and the no-touching restriction from sub-rule a (do not allow a primary target to touch another primary target or a beacon control slash). The card purports to summarize how target separation is measured on radar displays but presents an incomplete picture.
operations.source_basis: JO 7110.65 5-5-2 requires applying radar separation between centers of primary targets without allowing primary targets to touch each other or beacon control slashes, between ends of beacon control slashes, between end of a beacon control slash and center of a primary target, and between centers of digital targets without touching.

match.front: How is target separation measured on radar displays?
match.back: Separate primary targets center-to-center, beacon slashes end-to-end, and on all-digital displays keep digital targets center-to-center without letting them touch.

replacements.front: How is target separation measured on radar displays?
replacements.back: Primary targets: center-to-center, and do not allow a primary target to touch another primary target or a beacon control slash. Beacon control slashes: end-to-end. Beacon slash to primary target: end of slash to center of primary. All-digital displays: center-to-center, and do not allow digital targets to touch.

operations.source_basis: When severe turbulence is reported, aircraft are conducting military aerial refueling, or the aircraft previously at that altitude has been issued a climb or descent at pilot's discretion, wait until the first aircraft is observed at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minima before assigning its old altitude to another aircraft.

match.question_text: In which situation under 5-5-6 must you wait until the first aircraft is observed at or passing another altitude separated by the appropriate minima before assigning its old altitude to another aircraft?

The question asks about 'terminal operations' generally, and the correct answer specifies 'at least 15 degrees' — but 5-5-7a has two terminal sub-cases with different minima (15 degrees for ASR/FUSION, 45 degrees for ARSR/FUSION ISR). The answer is correct only for the ASR/FUSION sub-case. The explanation is underspecified: 'Passing or diverging separation has specific observation, divergence, and monitoring requirements' does not teach the rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-5-7a1(a): 'Single Site ASR or FUSION Mode ... one aircraft has crossed the projected course of the other, and the angular difference between their courses/assigned radar vectors is at least 15 degrees.' 5-5-7a1(b): 'The tracks are monitored to ensure that the primary targets, beacon control slashes, FUSION target symbols, or full digital terminal system primary and/or beacon target symbols will not touch.'

content.question_text: When may passing or diverging separation replace other approved separation in terminal operations?

choices.text: When the aircraft have passed or crossed as required, their courses or vectors diverge by at least 15 degrees, and the tracks are monitored so the applicable targets will not touch.
Whenever the aircraft are more than 1 mile apart and both pilots have each other in sight.
Only when the aircraft are on the same final approach course.
Only when the aircraft are climbing and descending through each other's altitude.

content.question_text: In terminal single-site ASR or FUSION mode, when may passing or diverging separation replace other approved separation?

choices.text: When one aircraft has crossed the projected course of the other with at least a 15-degree angular difference and the tracks are monitored so the targets will not touch.
When both pilots report each other in sight and the aircraft are more than 1 mile apart regardless of course geometry.
Only when the aircraft are established on the same final approach course and descending through each other's altitude.
When the aircraft are on parallel courses at the same altitude and at least 3 miles apart.

content.explanation: In single-site ASR or FUSION mode, other approved separation may be discontinued and passing or diverging separation applied when one aircraft has crossed the projected course of the other with at least a 15-degree angular difference and the tracks are monitored to ensure the targets will not touch.

operations.problem: The explanation merely restates the correct transmission: 'The correct transmission is REPORT PASSING THE TRAFFIC.' It does not teach the operational sequence — this instruction follows the pilot's affirmative traffic-in-sight report and precedes the passing report and subsequent control instructions. The phraseology_builder format and word bank are otherwise sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-5-7 example block: 'Traffic, twelve o'clock, Boeing Seven Twenty Seven, opposite direction. Do you have it in sight? (If the answer is in the affirmative): Report passing the traffic. (When pilot reports passing the traffic and the radar targets confirm that the traffic has passed, issue appropriate control instructions.)'

content.situation: A pilot has opposite-direction traffic in sight and you need a report once the traffic is passed. Build the instruction.
content.explanation: The correct transmission is "REPORT PASSING THE TRAFFIC."

content.situation: A pilot has opposite-direction traffic in sight and you need a report once the traffic is passed. Build the instruction.
content.explanation: After the pilot reports the traffic in sight, the controller instructs the pilot to report when the traffic has passed. Once the pilot reports passing and radar targets confirm the passing, appropriate control instructions follow.

operations.problem: The explanation cites 'Per the example in 5-5-7' — a paragraph-location reference that the student does not need. The readback_check format, clearance text, and answer choices are all sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-5-7 example block demonstrates the sequence: controller asks if traffic is in sight, pilot affirms, controller says 'Report passing the traffic,' pilot reports passing, radar confirms, control instructions follow.

content.instruction: The controller issued a traffic-report instruction. Which pilot readback is correct?

choices.text: You may discontinue the other approved separation and apply passing or diverging separation.

operations.problem: The back uses vague placeholder phrases — 'the required angle' and 'the required spacing increasing' — instead of stating the actual operational criteria. A flashcard back should deliver the specific minima and conditions the controller needs to recall, not paraphrase them with indefinite references.
operations.source_basis: 5-5-7a1(a): 15 degrees angular difference for terminal ASR/FUSION mode. 5-5-7a2(a): 45 degrees for ARSR/FUSION ISR mode. 5-5-7a1(b)/a2(b): tracks monitored to ensure targets will not touch. 5-5-7a3/b6: wake turbulence separation requirements still apply.

match.front: When can passing or diverging separation replace other approved separation?
match.back: Use it only after the required crossing or passing geometry exists, the courses diverge by the required angle, and the targets are monitored to keep the required spacing increasing.

replacements.front: When can passing or diverging separation replace other approved separation?
replacements.back: After the required crossing or passing geometry exists, the courses diverge by at least 15 degrees (terminal ASR/FUSION) or 45 degrees (ARSR/FUSION ISR), and the targets are monitored to ensure they will not touch. Wake turbulence separation requirements still apply when required.

operations.problem: The stem asks 'under 5-5-8' — paragraph-location scaffolding. The explanation also cites the paragraph. The question content and choices are otherwise correct.

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-1' and explanation begins with 'Per 5-6-1a', teaching paragraph lookup instead of the operational rule. The correct answer is substantially longer than all distractors, creating a visible length cue.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-1a: In controlled airspace for separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or when a pilot requests.

match.question_text: Under 5-6-1, in controlled airspace for which purpose may aircraft be vectored?

replacements.question_text: In controlled airspace, aircraft may be vectored for which of the following reasons?
replacements.explanation: In controlled airspace, ATC may vector aircraft for six specific purposes: separation from other traffic, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or when a pilot requests vectors. These are the controlling justifications; vectoring for any other reason is not authorized.

choices.text: For separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or when a pilot requests.
Only for traffic separation and safety; operational advantage and noise abatement are not valid reasons for vectoring.
Only when the aircraft is on an IFR flight plan and requires navigational guidance to rejoin a published route.
Only for noise abatement and confidence maneuvers; routine traffic separation does not justify vectoring.

operations.problem: Stem asks 'under 5-6-1' and explanation cites 'Per 5-6-1b', framing the item as a document-lookup exercise rather than an operational rule about Class G airspace limitations.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-1b: In Class G airspace only upon pilot request and as an additional service.

match.question_text: When may an aircraft be vectored in Class G airspace under 5-6-1?

replacements.question_text: When may a controller vector an aircraft in Class G airspace?
replacements.explanation: Vectoring in Class G airspace is restricted to pilot-requested additional service only. Unlike in controlled airspace, the controller may not initiate vectors in Class G at their own discretion — the pilot must first ask for the service.

choices.text: Only upon pilot request and as an additional service.
Whenever the controller determines a vector would improve traffic flow or sequencing.
Only when the aircraft is operating IFR and below the minimum vectoring altitude.
Class G airspace never permits ATC vectors under any circumstances.

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-1' and explanation cites 'Per 5-6-1d'. The true/false stem is a near-verbatim restatement of the source block, making the correct answer transparent without testing whether the controller understands the jurisdiction rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-1d: In airspace for which you have control jurisdiction, unless otherwise coordinated.

match.question_text: Under 5-6-1, aircraft may be vectored outside your control jurisdiction without coordination as long as radar contact is maintained.

replacements.question_text: A controller may vector an aircraft into another controller's airspace without coordinating first, provided radar contact is maintained.
replacements.explanation: Controllers must vector aircraft only within airspace for which they have control jurisdiction, unless coordination with the affected controller has been completed. Maintaining radar contact does not substitute for coordination when vectors cross jurisdictional boundaries.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-1' and explanation cites 'Per 5-6-1c'. The true/false stem restates the source text verbatim, requiring no operational reasoning. Converting to multiple-choice tests knowledge of which operations are excepted from the MVA/MIA floor.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-1c: At or above the MVA or the minimum IFR altitude except as authorized for radar approaches, radar departures, special VFR, VFR operations, or by paragraph 5-6-3, Vectors Below Minimum Altitude.

match.question_text: Under 5-6-1, vector aircraft at or above the MVA or minimum IFR altitude except in the specific authorized situations listed in the paragraph.

replacements.question_text: A controller may vector an aircraft below the MVA or minimum IFR altitude for which of the following operations?
replacements.explanation: The general rule requires vectors at or above the MVA or minimum IFR altitude. However, radar approaches, radar departures, special VFR, VFR operations, and vectors-below-minimum-altitude procedures are specific authorized exceptions to the altitude floor.

choices.text: Radar approaches, radar departures, special VFR, VFR operations, or vectors-below-minimum-altitude procedures.
Only emergency descents and weather deviations, regardless of any other operational need.
Any IFR aircraft whenever the pilot accepts responsibility for terrain and obstacle clearance.
Only when the aircraft is operating in Class G airspace, where MVA protections do not apply.

operations.problem: Explanation reads 'Per 5-6-2, the prescribed phraseology begins with TURN' — it cites a paragraph number rather than teaching the phraseology method for issuing heading vectors. The fill_blank format is appropriate for exact phraseology recall.

match.question_text: Complete the prescribed vector phraseology: "_____ LEFT/RIGHT HEADING DEGREES"

replacements.question_text: Complete the prescribed vector phraseology: "_____ LEFT/RIGHT HEADING (degrees)"
replacements.explanation: The standard vector instruction begins with the word TURN when specifying a direction of turn and a heading to be flown. This forms the prescribed phraseology TURN LEFT/RIGHT HEADING (degrees), the first of several approved vector methods.

choices.text: TURN

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-2' and explanation cites 'Per the note under 5-6-2d'. The correct answer is substantially longer than all distractors, creating a visible length cue.
operations.source_basis: Note under 5-6-2d: After a climb via or descend via clearance has been issued, a vector or deviation off of a SID or STAR cancels all published altitude and speed restrictions on the procedure. The aircraft's Flight Management System may be unable to process crossing altitude restrictions once the aircraft leaves the SID or STAR lateral path.

match.question_text: Under 5-6-2, what happens to published altitude and speed restrictions on a SID or STAR after a climb via or descend via clearance has been issued and the aircraft is vectored off the procedure?

replacements.question_text: After a climb via or descend via clearance has been issued, what happens to a SID or STAR's published altitude and speed restrictions when the aircraft is vectored off the procedure?
replacements.explanation: Once a climb via or descend via clearance has been issued, any vector or deviation off the SID or STAR lateral path cancels all published altitude and speed restrictions on that procedure. The aircraft's FMS cannot process crossing restrictions once it leaves the lateral path, so an explicit altitude must be assigned.

choices.text: All published altitude and speed restrictions on the procedure are canceled.
Only speed restrictions are canceled; altitude restrictions remain effective because the FMS continues to enforce them.
Only altitude restrictions are canceled; published speeds remain mandatory to protect sector traffic flow.
No restrictions are affected unless the pilot independently requests cancellation of each individual restriction.

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-2' and explanation cites 'Per 5-6-2f'. The correct answer is substantially longer than all distractors, creating a visible length cue.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-2f: Aircraft instructed to resume a procedure which contains published crossing restrictions (SID or STAR) must be issued or reissued all applicable restrictions or be instructed to Climb Via or Descend Via.

match.question_text: Under 5-6-2, when an aircraft is instructed to resume a SID or STAR that contains published crossing restrictions, what must ATC do?

replacements.question_text: When an aircraft is instructed to resume a SID or STAR that contains published crossing restrictions, what must ATC do?
replacements.explanation: Published crossing restrictions on a SID or STAR do not automatically reactivate when an aircraft resumes the procedure after being vectored off. ATC must either issue or reissue all applicable restrictions, or instruct the aircraft to Climb Via or Descend Via the procedure.

choices.text: Issue or reissue all applicable crossing restrictions, or instruct the aircraft to Climb Via or Descend Via the procedure.
Simply clear the aircraft to resume the procedure, because crossing restrictions automatically reactivate when the aircraft rejoins the lateral path.
Issue only the next crossing restriction ahead of the aircraft; already-passed waypoints with restrictions need not be repeated.
The procedure is permanently canceled after any vector off it; the aircraft must be recleared to every waypoint individually.

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-2' and explanation says only 'That requirement appears in 5-6-2g' — a paragraph citation that teaches nothing about why the ODP protection exists or what consequence follows from the vector.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-2g: Aircraft may not be vectored off an Obstacle Departure Procedure, or issued an altitude lower than published altitude on an ODP, until at or above the MVA or MIA, at which time the ODP is canceled. Note: Once an aircraft has been vectored off an ODP, the procedure is canceled and ATC cannot clear the aircraft to resume the ODP.

match.question_text: Under 5-6-2, aircraft may not be vectored off an Obstacle Departure Procedure until at or above the MVA or MIA.

replacements.question_text: An aircraft may be vectored off an Obstacle Departure Procedure only after reaching at or above the MVA or MIA.
replacements.explanation: ODPs provide programmed obstacle clearance from the departure runway. An aircraft may not be vectored off an ODP, or issued an altitude lower than the published ODP altitude, until it reaches the MVA or MIA. Once vectored off, the ODP is canceled and cannot be resumed.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-3 terminal procedures' and explanation cites 'Per 5-6-3a2', making the item a paragraph lookup rather than a test of the obstacle-separation rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-3a2: The flight path is less than 3 miles from the obstacle and the aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle, vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation from the obstacle until the 3 mile minimum is achieved or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.

match.question_text: Under 5-6-3 terminal procedures, if the flight path is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle and the aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle, what must ATC do?

replacements.question_text: When a departing IFR aircraft's flight path is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle and ISR is not displayed, what must the controller do while the aircraft climbs to at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle?
replacements.explanation: When the flight path is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle, the controller must actively increase lateral separation from the obstacle. This continues until the 3-mile minimum is achieved or until the aircraft reports it has left an altitude above the obstacle. A sub-3-mile path is not acceptable even when the aircraft is climbing.

choices.text: Vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation until 3 miles exists or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.
Maintain the current track, because the aircraft's climb toward 1,000 feet above the obstacle satisfies the lateral separation requirement.
Descend the aircraft below the obstacle's height so lateral clearance can be independently verified by radar.
Transfer navigation and obstacle avoidance responsibility to the pilot once the aircraft is above the obstacle's published elevation.

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under 5-6-3a3' and explanation cites 'Per 5-6-3a3'. The correct answer is substantially longer than all distractors, creating a visible length cue.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-3a3: Radar facilities may vector aircraft below the MVA or MIA, provided: (a) No prominent obstacles are within 10 NM of the departure end of runway. (b) Aircraft must be allowed an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER.

match.question_text: When may radar facilities vector aircraft below the MVA or MIA under 5-6-3a3?

replacements.question_text: Under what conditions may a radar facility vector a departing aircraft below the MVA or MIA when ISR is not displayed?
replacements.explanation: Two conditions must both be met for below-MVA/MIA vectoring: no prominent obstacles may exist within 10 NM of the departure end of the runway, and the aircraft must be allowed an uninterrupted climb path to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER.

choices.text: No prominent obstacles within 10 NM of the departure end of the runway, and an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER.
Any time the pilot requests a shortcut after departure and accepts visual separation from terrain.
Only after the aircraft has already climbed above the MVA or MIA for the first route segment.
Whenever both a DVA and a radar SID are published for the same departure runway simultaneously.

operations.problem: Stem begins with 'Under the note in 5-6-3' and explanation states only 'That statement appears directly in the note under 5-6-3' — a paragraph citation that does not teach when ATC assumes or does not assume terrain and obstacle responsibility.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-3 Note: ATC assumes responsibility for terrain and obstacle avoidance when IFR aircraft are below the minimum IFR altitude and are taken off departure or missed approach procedures, or if issued go-around instructions, except after conducting a visual approach. ATC does not assume this responsibility when utilizing a DVA or when operating on SIDs with or without a published range of headings.

match.question_text: Under the note in 5-6-3, when IFR aircraft below minimum IFR altitude are taken off departure or missed approach procedures, ATC assumes responsibility for terrain and obstacle avoidance except as stated for DVAs and SIDs.

replacements.question_text: When an IFR aircraft below minimum IFR altitude is taken off a departure or missed approach procedure, when does ATC NOT assume responsibility for terrain and obstacle avoidance?
replacements.explanation: ATC assumes terrain and obstacle avoidance responsibility when IFR aircraft below minimum IFR altitude are taken off a departure or missed approach procedure, except in three cases: after a visual approach, when using a Diverse Vector Area, or when operating on SIDs with or without a published range of headings.

choices.text: After conducting a visual approach, when utilizing a Diverse Vector Area, or when operating on SIDs with or without a published heading range.
ATC always retains terrain and obstacle avoidance responsibility for every IFR aircraft below the MVA without exception.
Only when the aircraft is in Class G airspace, where terrain separation responsibility rests entirely with the pilot.
Whenever the pilot has filed an IFR flight plan that includes a published departure procedure.

operations.problem: The para_context field opens with 'Under 5-6-1' and the explanation begins with 'Per 5-6-1b', making the activity a paragraph lookup. The situation itself is a well-constructed operational dilemma.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-1b: In Class G airspace only upon pilot request and as an additional service.

content.situation: A VFR aircraft is flying in Class G airspace and has not requested vectors. Another controller wants to turn it for sequencing anyway because the traffic picture would be simpler if the aircraft joined a more convenient path.
content.para_context: Under 5-6-1, vector aircraft in controlled airspace for the listed ATC purposes, but in Class G airspace only upon pilot request and as an additional service. Vector only in airspace for which you have control jurisdiction and at or above the MVA or MIA except where specific exceptions apply.

choices.text: Do not vector the Class G aircraft unless the pilot requests vectors; Class G vectoring is an additional service provided only on pilot request.
Vector the aircraft for sequencing whenever it improves traffic flow, because Class G vectoring is available at controller discretion.
Vector the aircraft first and then ask whether the pilot wanted the additional service after the turn is complete.
Refuse any vectoring in Class G even if the pilot later requests it, because vectors are never provided outside controlled airspace.

content.explanation: Per 5-6-1b, vectoring in Class G airspace is provided only on pilot request and as an additional service.

content.situation: A VFR aircraft is flying in Class G airspace and has not requested vectors. Another controller wants to turn it for sequencing anyway because the traffic picture would be simpler if the aircraft joined a more convenient path.
content.para_context: Vector aircraft in controlled airspace for ATC purposes including separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or pilot request. In Class G airspace, provide vectors only upon pilot request and as an additional service. Vector only in airspace for which you have control jurisdiction and at or above the MVA or MIA except where specific exceptions apply.

choices.text: Do not vector the Class G aircraft unless the pilot requests vectors; Class G vectoring is an additional service provided only on pilot request.
Vector the aircraft for sequencing whenever it improves traffic flow, because Class G vectoring is available at controller discretion.
Vector the aircraft first and then ask whether the pilot wanted the additional service after the turn is complete.
Refuse any vectoring in Class G even if the pilot later requests it, because vectors are never provided outside controlled airspace.

content.explanation: Class G vectoring is limited to pilot-requested additional service. A controller may not initiate vectors in Class G airspace for traffic sequencing or convenience; the pilot must first request the service before any vector may be issued.

operations.problem: Explanation begins with 'Per 5-6-2d', teaching a paragraph reference rather than the operational rule about advising the pilot of intent to resume a procedure after vectoring.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-2d: When vectoring or approving an aircraft to deviate off of a procedure, advise the pilot if you intend on clearing the aircraft to resume the procedure.

content.clearance: FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO, MAINTAIN SEVEN THOUSAND, EXPECT TO RESUME THE KSINO ONE ARRIVAL.

choices.text: Fly heading zero niner zero, maintain seven thousand, expect to resume the Ksino One arrival, N123AB.
Fly heading zero niner zero, maintain seven thousand, expect to cancel the Ksino One arrival, N123AB.
Fly heading zero niner zero, descend via the Ksino One arrival, N123AB.
Resume own navigation to the arrival now, N123AB.

content.explanation: Per 5-6-2d, when vectoring or approving an aircraft off a procedure and intending to clear the aircraft to resume it, advise the pilot accordingly.

content.clearance: FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO, MAINTAIN SEVEN THOUSAND, EXPECT TO RESUME THE KSINO ONE ARRIVAL.

choices.text: Fly heading zero niner zero, maintain seven thousand, expect to resume the Ksino One arrival, N123AB.
Fly heading zero niner zero, maintain seven thousand, expect to cancel the Ksino One arrival, N123AB.
Fly heading zero niner zero, descend via the Ksino One arrival, N123AB.
Resume own navigation to the arrival now, N123AB.

content.explanation: When a controller vectors an aircraft off a procedure and intends to clear the aircraft to resume it later, the transmission must include the expectation of resuming the procedure. The pilot must read back the intent to resume, not a cancellation or a different clearance entirely.

operations.problem: Explanation states 'Per 5-6-2d, the advisory is EXPECT TO RESUME the SID, STAR, or procedure' — a paragraph citation rather than instruction on the deviation advisory phraseology rule.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-2d: When vectoring or approving an aircraft to deviate off of a procedure, advise the pilot if you intend on clearing the aircraft to resume the procedure.

content.explanation: Per 5-6-2d, the advisory is EXPECT TO RESUME the SID, STAR, or procedure.

content.display_text: FLY HEADING ZERO NINER ZERO MAINTAIN SEVEN THOUSAND EXPECT TO CANCEL THE KSINO ONE ARRIVAL
content.explanation: When vectoring an aircraft off a procedure, the controller advises the pilot to expect to resume the procedure, not to cancel it. The word CANCEL is incorrect; the correct prescribed word is RESUME.

operations.problem: The correct answer is substantially longer than all distractors, creating a visible length cue. The explanation is generic — it states the provision has 'specific conditions' without naming them, missing the opportunity to teach the two controlling requirements.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-3a3: Radar facilities may vector aircraft below the MVA or MIA, provided: (a) No prominent obstacles are within 10 NM of the departure end of runway. (b) Aircraft must be allowed an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER.

content.question_text: After departure or a missed approach, when may a radar facility vector an aircraft below the MVA or MIA under the special provision?

choices.text: No prominent obstacles may be within 10 NM of the departure end of runway, and the aircraft must be allowed an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM.
The pilot must agree to maintain visual separation from terrain.
Any vector below the MVA or MIA is allowed if the aircraft is on a SID.
The aircraft must already be above the MEA for the first route segment.

content.explanation: The below-minimum vectoring provision has specific obstacle and climb conditions; it is not a general shortcut.

content.question_text: After departure or a missed approach, when may a radar facility vector an aircraft below the MVA or MIA under the special provision?

choices.text: No prominent obstacles within 10 NM of the DER, and an uninterrupted climb to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER.
The pilot must accept visual responsibility for terrain and obstacle clearance throughout the departure.
The aircraft must be on a published SID with radar vectors depicted in the departure route description.
The MEA for the first en route segment must already be below the aircraft's current altitude.

content.explanation: The below-MVA/MIA vectoring provision requires two conditions: no prominent obstacles within 10 NM of the departure end of the runway, and an uninterrupted climb path that allows the aircraft to meet the MVA or MIA within 10 NM of the DER. Both conditions must be satisfied before vectors below minimum altitude are authorized.

operations.problem: The para_context field begins with 'Under 5-6-3a2' and the explanation begins with 'Per 5-6-3a2', teaching paragraph lookup instead of the obstacle-separation decision rule. The situation itself is a realistic operational dilemma.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-3a2: The flight path is less than 3 miles from the obstacle and the aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle, vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation from the obstacle until the 3 mile minimum is achieved or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.

content.situation: A departing IFR aircraft is below the MVA, ISR is not displayed, and the flight path is about 2 miles from a prominent obstacle. The aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above that obstacle. Another controller says 2 miles is close enough until the aircraft gets higher.
content.para_context: Under 5-6-3a2, if the flight path is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle and the aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle, vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation until the 3-mile minimum is achieved or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.

choices.text: Vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation from the obstacle until 3 miles is achieved or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.
Leave the aircraft 2 miles from the obstacle because any climb toward 1,000 feet above it removes the 3-mile requirement.
Descend the aircraft to keep it clear of the obstacle laterally until ISR appears in the data block.
Cancel IFR and let the pilot maintain obstacle separation visually until the aircraft reaches the MVA.

content.explanation: Per 5-6-3a2, when the aircraft is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle, ATC increases lateral separation until 3 miles exists or the aircraft reports above the obstacle.

content.situation: A departing IFR aircraft is below the MVA, ISR is not displayed, and the flight path is about 2 miles from a prominent obstacle. The aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above that obstacle. Another controller says 2 miles is close enough until the aircraft gets higher.
content.para_context: When a departing IFR aircraft's flight path is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle, ISR is not displayed, and the aircraft is climbing to at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle, the controller must vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation from the obstacle until the 3-mile minimum is achieved or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.

choices.text: Vector the aircraft to increase lateral separation from the obstacle until 3 miles is achieved or until the aircraft reports leaving an altitude above the obstacle.
Leave the aircraft 2 miles from the obstacle because any climb toward 1,000 feet above it removes the 3-mile requirement.
Descend the aircraft to keep it clear of the obstacle laterally until ISR appears in the data block.
Cancel IFR and let the pilot maintain obstacle separation visually until the aircraft reaches the MVA.

content.explanation: When the flight path is less than 3 miles from a prominent obstacle, the controller must actively increase lateral separation until the 3-mile minimum is achieved. This requirement remains in effect until the aircraft reports it has climbed above the obstacle. Leaving the aircraft at 2 miles while waiting for more altitude does not satisfy the separation requirement.

operations.problem: The back of the card is vague and self-referential — 'as described in facility directives and the paragraph's lateral or climb protections' points to external text rather than providing retrievable content from memory. A flashcard back must be a self-contained retrieval target.
operations.source_basis: 5-6-3a: Terminal. As described in facility directives, when vectoring a departing IFR aircraft, or one executing a missed approach, when ISR is not displayed in the full data block and before it reaches the minimum altitude for IFR operations if separation from prominent obstacles shown on the radar scope is applied in accordance with one of the following three conditions.

match.front: When may a terminal controller vector below minimum IFR altitude?
match.back: Only under the listed obstacle-clearance conditions for departing IFR aircraft or missed approaches, as described in facility directives and the paragraph's lateral or climb protections.

replacements.front: When may a terminal controller vector below minimum IFR altitude?
replacements.back: When vectoring a departing IFR or missed approach aircraft with ISR not displayed, under one of three obstacle-separation conditions: maintain at least 3 NM laterally from obstacles while climbing 1,000 ft above them; increase separation until 3 NM exists; or no prominent obstacles within 10 NM of DER with uninterrupted climb to MVA/MIA within 10 NM.

operations.problem: The stem opens with a paragraph citation ('Under 5-7-1') and uses negative framing ('must not be assigned without pilot consent'). The explanation cites a sub-paragraph label instead of teaching the operational distinction between the FL390 consent requirement and the outright prohibitions that follow in the same list.
operations.source_basis: 5-7-1 note block sequence 1: 'Do not assign speed adjustment to aircraft: 1. At or above FL 390 without pilot consent. 2. Executing a published high altitude instrument approach procedure. 3. In a holding pattern.' Only sub-item 1 carries the 'without pilot consent' qualifier; sub-items 2 and 3 are absolute prohibitions where consent does not apply.

match.question_text: Under 5-7-1, to which aircraft must speed adjustment not be assigned without pilot consent?

replacements.question_text: For which aircraft is pilot consent required before assigning a speed adjustment?
replacements.explanation: Speed adjustments must not be assigned to aircraft at or above FL 390 without pilot consent. This differs from aircraft executing a published high altitude instrument approach procedure or aircraft in a holding pattern, to which speed adjustments are simply not assigned — consent does not override those prohibitions.

choices.text: An aircraft at or above FL 390.
An aircraft executing a published high altitude instrument approach procedure.
An aircraft below 10,000 feet MSL on a visual approach.
An aircraft in a holding pattern.

operations.problem: The stem opens with a paragraph citation ('Under 5-7-1') and the explanation merely states where the requirement appears ('That requirement appears in 5-7-1c and 5-7-1d') instead of teaching the operational effect: approach and via clearances cancel previously assigned speeds, so a controller must restate the speed if it is still needed.
operations.source_basis: 5-7-1 reference block sequence 2: 'At the time approach clearance or a climb via/descend via clearance is issued, previously assigned speeds must be restated if required. Approach clearances or climb via/descend via clearances cancel any previously assigned speeds. Pilots are expected to make their own speed adjustments to fly the approach, SID, or STAR unless assigned speeds are restated.'

match.question_text: Under 5-7-1, approach clearances or climb via or descend via clearances cancel previously assigned speeds unless the speeds are restated.

replacements.question_text: Approach clearances and climb via/descend via clearances cancel previously assigned speeds unless the speeds are restated.
replacements.explanation: An approach clearance or a climb via/descend via clearance cancels any previously assigned speed. Pilots will then make their own speed adjustments to fly the procedure. If the controller still requires the assigned speed, it must be restated at the time the clearance is issued.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The explanation cites a sub-paragraph label ('Per 5-7-1e') instead of stating the operational rule directly. The question statement is slightly imprecise ('you must treat... as canceled') when the source states the restriction is canceled as a matter of rule. The choices are otherwise sound.
operations.source_basis: 5-7-1 note block sequence 3: 'A speed restriction published as part of a SID/STAR is canceled when an aircraft is vectored off, or a deviation from the SID/STAR is approved. If necessary, assign a speed in conjunction with the vector or approval to deviate.'

content.question_text: When an aircraft is vectored off a SID or STAR, you must treat the published speed restriction on that procedure as canceled.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: Per 5-7-1e, a published speed restriction on a SID or STAR is canceled when the aircraft is vectored off or approved to deviate from the procedure.

content.question_text: When an aircraft is vectored off a SID or STAR, the published speed restriction on that procedure is canceled.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: Vectoring an aircraft off the SID or STAR cancels the published speed restriction. If spacing still requires a speed, the controller must assign one in conjunction with the vector or the approval to deviate.

operations.problem: The explanation merely locates the directive ('That directive appears in the opening sentence of 5-7-1') without teaching why termination is required. The question statement and choices are sound.
